High-EPA omega-3 as an adjunct — modest antidepressant signal, not a standalone fix

causes.confusedWith: Hypothyroidism and iron deficiency, which share the fatigue-plus-low-mood picture and must be excluded by labs; and genuine ME/CFS, defined by post-exertional malaise rather than low mood.
causes.keyInsight: When your mind is under sustained stress or low mood, your body's built-in alarm system gets stuck in the "on" position — and a brain running its emergency system around the clock has no spare energy left for the ordinary business of caring, focusing, or feeling rested.
causes.hook: Tired, flat, and nothing feels worth the effort — worst by morning.
causes.evidence: This is a well-established mechanism: the link between persistent stress, low mood, and the body's stress-and-inflammation systems is one of the most heavily studied pathways in medicine. The exact chain from mood to inflammation is still partly a strong association rather than a fully nailed-down cause, but the overall pattern — that a chronically activated stress system drains energy — is firmly established.
causes.plain: Long-running stress, grief, or burnout keeps your body's stress-response system switched on far longer than it was designed for, and that system runs on a stress hormone called cortisol (a chemical messenger carried in your blood) plus low-level inflammation (your immune system idling in a mild "alarm" state). Kept elevated for weeks or months, these signals quietly turn down the brain circuits that generate motivation and pleasure, and they scramble the deep, restorative stages of your sleep. The result is a specific kind of exhaustion: fatigue that sleep doesn't fix, a flatness where interest used to be, and trouble concentrating. It often feels worst in the morning because that is exactly when your stress hormone naturally peaks. The good news is that this is a system stuck in a loop, not permanent damage — and loops can be interrupted.

causes.name: Blood-sugar swings & insulin resistance

chain.node: Refined-carb meals low in fiber and protein
chain.lay: You eat fast carbs with little to slow them down
chain.say: It starts with **refined carbohydrates** — these are sugary or starchy foods that have had most of their natural fiber stripped out, like white bread, white rice, pastries, sweet drinks, and most snack foods. **Carbohydrates** are simply the family of foods your body breaks down into sugar for fuel, and **fiber** is the tough, indigestible part of plants that normally acts like a speed bump, slowing how fast that sugar enters your blood. **Protein** — the building-block nutrient found in eggs, meat, fish, beans, and dairy — also slows digestion down. So when a meal is high in refined carbs but low in both fiber and protein, there is nothing to put the brakes on: the sugar is set up to arrive in your bloodstream all at once. This first step is just the setup — the trigger that makes everything that follows possible.

chain.node: Glucose spike triggers an exaggerated, overshooting insulin surge
chain.lay: Blood sugar shoots up, so your body overreacts with insulin
chain.say: Because that meal had nothing to slow it down, the sugar rushes in and your **blood glucose** — the amount of sugar circulating in your blood — spikes sharply upward. Your body treats a high blood-sugar level as something to clean up quickly, so it calls in **insulin**, a **hormone** (a chemical messenger that travels through your blood to tell other parts of your body what to do) made by your **pancreas**, an organ tucked behind your stomach. Insulin's job is to move sugar out of your blood and into your cells to be used or stored, which brings the level back down. But here's the catch: when the spike is big and fast, your pancreas tends to **overshoot**, releasing more insulin than was actually needed. That oversized insulin response is what sets up the fall in the next step — you don't just come back down gently, you get pushed down hard.

chain.node: A rapid glucose decline (true reactive hypoglycemia is uncommon) plus the post-spike insulin swing dips brain alertness
chain.lay: Sugar drops fast and your brain's fuel and alertness dip
chain.say: Because your body released more insulin than the moment actually called for, that surplus keeps pulling sugar out of your blood even after the level is already back to normal — so instead of levelling off gently, your blood glucose slides down quickly. Here's why that matters: your **brain** runs almost entirely on blood sugar for fuel, and it is unusually sensitive not just to how much fuel it has but to how fast that fuel is changing. So the combination of a fast-falling sugar level and the insulin that spiked high and is now settling back down — a rapid up-then-down on both sides — nudges your **alertness**, meaning how awake, sharp, and focused you feel, downward. It's worth being honest here: your blood sugar usually doesn't actually fall below the normal range (a genuine below-normal dip, called **reactive hypoglycemia**, is uncommon), so this is less about your brain running out of fuel and more about the sharp *change* itself unsettling it. In other words, the swing is the problem, not a real fuel shortage — and that dip in alertness is exactly what you're about to feel.

chain.node: Post-meal crash: sleepy, foggy, sometimes shaky, hungry again within hours
chain.lay: The crash hits: sleepy, foggy, shaky, hungry again
chain.say: Because your brain's alertness has just been dragged down by that rapid sugar drop and insulin swing, this is the moment the **post-meal crash** actually shows up as a feeling. You get drowsy and heavy-eyed, your thinking turns **foggy** — slow and hard to focus, like your mind is wading through mud — and some people feel faintly **shaky** as the body registers the swing. On top of that you often feel hungry again within a couple of hours, even though you ate recently, because the same overshoot that dropped your sugar also nudges your appetite back on. This is the afternoon slump after a starchy lunch, or the flatness that follows a sugary snack — and the reason higher-protein, lower-carb meals leave you steadier is simply that they never trigger the big spike that starts this whole chain.

tell.symptoms: Your energy nosedives one to three hours after a carb-heavy or sugary meal — a classic afternoon slump after a starchy lunch — and you often feel foggy or ravenously hungry again between meals. You tend to feel noticeably steadier and clearer on days when you eat more protein and fewer refined carbs.
tell.labMarker: Fasting glucose and HbA1c drifting toward the prediabetic range (HbA1c 5.7%+); elevated fasting insulin or HOMA-IR. A CGM showing sharp spikes followed by rapid drops is the clearest signal — but note documented sub-70 mg/dL hypoglycemia is actually rare.

fixes.what: Eat protein and fiber before or instead of refined carbs; cut sugary drinks and snacks
Take a 10-15 min walk after meals to blunt the glucose spike
Alpha-lipoic acid may support insulin sensitivity and glucose handling (modest evidence)
Magnesium — low levels track with insulin resistance

causes.confusedWith: Simple postprandial somnolence — a normal, mild 'food coma' from any large meal that doesn't come with shakiness or repeated between-meal crashes.
causes.keyInsight: A fast sugar rush doesn't leave you high — it leaves you flat, because your body slams the brakes so hard it overshoots, and the crash that follows is what you actually feel as tiredness.
causes.hook: Sleepy and foggy an hour or two after eating carbs?
causes.evidence: This is a tier 2 cause: a strong, well-documented association. The link between blood-sugar swings, insulin overshoot, and post-meal drops in alertness is measured repeatedly in research, though how strongly any one person feels it varies, and true reactive hypoglycemia (blood sugar actually dropping below normal) is uncommon.
causes.plain: When you eat fast-digesting carbohydrates with little fiber or protein to slow them down, the sugar from that food floods into your blood quickly. Your body reacts by releasing insulin, the hormone that clears sugar out of the blood — but after a big fast spike it often releases too much, so your blood sugar swings back down rapidly. Your brain runs almost entirely on blood sugar for fuel and is sensitive to these swings, so as the level dips and the insulin surge settles, your alertness dips with it. The result is the familiar post-meal crash: sleepy, foggy, sometimes a little shaky, and hungry again within a couple of hours. Meals built around protein, fiber, and fat blunt the initial spike, which is why steadier eating tends to mean steadier energy.

causes.name: Hypothyroidism (overt; subclinical is contested)

chain.node: Autoimmune thyroid damage (Hashimoto's), or iodine/selenium inadequacy
chain.lay: Your thyroid gland gets damaged or starved of what it needs
chain.say: Your **thyroid** is a small, butterfly-shaped **gland** — an organ whose job is to make and release chemical messengers — sitting at the front of your neck. The most common thing that goes wrong is an **autoimmune** attack, meaning your own immune system (the defence force that normally fights germs) mistakenly treats your thyroid as an enemy and slowly damages it; this specific version is called **Hashimoto's**. The other route is simple shortage: the thyroid needs two raw materials from your diet — **iodine** and **selenium**, both minerals you get from food — to build its hormones, and if either runs low, it can't keep production up. Either way, the factory ends up crippled. That sets up everything that follows, because a damaged or under-supplied thyroid simply cannot make enough of its product.

chain.node: Falling thyroid hormone drives TSH up; less active T3 reaches tissues
chain.lay: Thyroid hormone drops, the body shouts for more, but tissues still go short
chain.say: Because your thyroid can no longer keep up, the level of **thyroid hormone** in your blood starts to fall — this is the messenger chemical that tells your cells how fast to run. Your brain notices the shortfall and responds by shouting louder: it pumps out more of a signal called **TSH** (thyroid-stimulating hormone), which is essentially the brain nagging the thyroid to work harder, so a high TSH on a blood test is a red flag that the gland is struggling. But nagging a broken factory doesn't fix it, so despite the loud signal, less of the **active** form of the hormone — called **T3**, the version your cells can actually use — reaches your **tissues** (the body's various working materials, like muscle, skin and gut). In short, because the supply has genuinely dried up, no amount of internal shouting can get enough working hormone to where it's needed.

chain.node: Slowed cellular metabolic rate and reduced Na/K-ATPase activity everywhere
chain.lay: Every cell slows down and its tiny pumps go quiet
chain.say: Now that too little active **T3** is reaching your **tissues**, the effect shows up inside your individual **cells** — the microscopic building blocks that make up every part of you. T3's normal job is to set each cell's **metabolic rate**, meaning how fast it burns fuel to generate energy and heat; with the signal weak, that rate slows right down and cells idle instead of running at full speed. One specific casualty is a tiny protein pump embedded in the cell wall called **Na/K-ATPase** — think of it as a busy little machine that constantly shuttles salt in and out of the cell, and running it burns a lot of fuel and throws off warmth as a by-product. Because T3 normally keeps these pumps active, less T3 means the pumps go quiet across your whole body at once. The overall picture is a body running in low-power mode everywhere, generating less energy and less heat than it should.

chain.node: Fatigue, cold intolerance, weight gain, sluggish mood and bowels
chain.lay: You feel it as tiredness, cold, weight gain and everything running slow
chain.say: All of that slowed-down cellular machinery is exactly what you feel from the outside. Because your cells are generating less usable energy, you feel **fatigue** — a deep, persistent tiredness that rest doesn't fully fix. Because those quiet salt pumps and idling cells produce less internal heat, you get **cold intolerance**, feeling chilly when everyone around you is fine. Because you're burning fuel more slowly, calories that used to be spent now linger, showing up as gradual, **unexplained weight gain**, while the same body-wide slowdown drags on your brain and gut too — leaving your **mood** flat and low and your bowels sluggish and **constipated**. Every one of these is the same single problem — a turned-down metabolic dial — simply being felt in a different part of your body.

tell.symptoms: You feel cold when the people around you are perfectly comfortable, your skin is dry and your bowels are sluggish, and the scale is creeping up even though you haven't changed how you eat. You may also notice puffiness, a low flat mood, a slow heartbeat, or the outer third of your eyebrows quietly thinning.
tell.labMarker: TSH elevated: overt hypothyroidism means TSH high (often >10 mIU/L) WITH low free T4 — this is the form that reliably causes fatigue and responds to treatment. Subclinical (TSH ~4.5-10, normal free T4) is contested: RCTs show levothyroxine usually does NOT relieve fatigue, especially over 65. Add TPO antibodies to check for Hashimoto's.

fixes.what: Get a full thyroid panel (TSH, free T4, TPO antibodies) — this is a diagnosis you can't fix blind
Levothyroxine replacement for overt hypothyroidism; for subclinical, don't expect fatigue relief — trial only if clearly symptomatic (physician-guided)
Ensure adequate selenium and iodine — Brazil nuts, seafood, eggs, dairy

causes.confusedWith: Depression and anemia, which share the fatigue-plus-low-mood picture; a TSH test separates them.
causes.keyInsight: Your thyroid sets the "pace" of every cell in your body — when it runs slow, everything runs slow, so your warmth, weight, mood, gut, and energy all dim at once from the same low setting.
causes.hook: Cold when no one else is, and always tired?
causes.evidence: This is a well-established, textbook mechanism: when the thyroid is clearly underactive (called "overt" hypothyroidism), the link to fatigue is straightforward physiology, and replacing the missing hormone reliably lifts energy. The milder "subclinical" version — where blood tests are only slightly off — is genuinely contested, and not everyone with borderline numbers feels better with treatment.
causes.plain: Your thyroid is a small gland in your neck that releases hormones controlling how fast your whole body runs. In hypothyroidism it makes too little of these hormones, so your cells slow down their internal machinery, produce less heat, and burn through fuel more lazily. The result is a cluster of symptoms that all point the same direction — cold, tired, heavy, slow — because a single dial has been turned down across every tissue at once. A simple blood test can usually confirm it, and when it's clearly present, replacing the missing hormone tends to reverse the fatigue.

causes.name: Mitochondrial inefficiency & cofactor depletion

chain.node: Aging, statin use, oxidative stress, or depleted CoQ10 / B-vitamin cofactors
chain.lay: Your cells' power plants lose the helper parts they need to run
chain.say: Deep inside nearly every one of your cells sit hundreds of tiny structures called **mitochondria** — think of them as microscopic power plants whose only job is to make energy for the cell. To do that work, they rely on a small set of helper molecules called **cofactors**, which are basically the spark plugs and lubricants of the engine; the two big ones here are **CoQ10** (a vitamin-like substance your body makes and also gets from food) and several **B-vitamins**. Over time these helpers can run low: normal **aging** slowly reduces how much CoQ10 your body produces, **statins** — the common cholesterol-lowering pills — happen to block the same chemical pathway your body uses to build CoQ10, and ongoing **oxidative stress** (a kind of internal rusting from unstable molecules called free radicals) wears the helpers down faster than you replace them. When any of these drains your cofactor supply, the power plants are left short of the exact parts they need to run at full speed. That shortage is the starting domino for everything that follows.

chain.node: Impaired electron transport chain lowers ATP output per unit of fuel
chain.lay: With parts missing, the power plants squeeze less energy out of each bit of fuel
chain.say: Because those helper molecules just ran low, the main energy assembly line inside each mitochondrion can no longer run cleanly. That assembly line is called the **electron transport chain** — a row of protein machines (proteins are the tiny worker-molecules that do most jobs in the body) that passes tiny electrical charges called **electrons** down the line, and CoQ10 is literally one of the shuttles that carries electrons between the machines. The payoff at the end of this line is **ATP**, which is simply the molecule your cells spend as energy — the actual cash currency that powers every muscle twitch and every thought. When CoQ10 and the B-vitamin helpers are depleted, the shuttles and machines can't keep the flow going, so the same amount of food and oxygen produces noticeably less ATP than it should. In plain terms, you're still putting fuel in the tank, but the engine is now converting less of it into usable power.

chain.node: Reduced ATP supply in high-demand muscle and brain tissue
chain.lay: The hungriest tissues — muscle and brain — get shortchanged first
chain.say: Now that each power plant is making less ATP per unit of fuel, the tissues that burn the most energy feel the pinch soonest. A **tissue** is just a group of similar cells working together, and your **muscle** and **brain** are the two most energy-hungry tissues you own — muscle because contracting takes constant ATP, and the brain because it never switches off. When the ATP supply dips, these high-demand tissues are the first to notice the shortfall, the way the biggest appliances in your house are the first to struggle when the power sags. It isn't that you have less fuel or less oxygen coming in — your iron and thyroid are normal, remember — it's that the energy actually being produced from that fuel falls short of what these demanding tissues need. That mismatch between demand and supply is what you're about to feel directly.

chain.node: Still drained despite good sleep and normal iron/thyroid; poor stamina and slow recovery
chain.lay: You feel drained and recover slowly even though nothing else is wrong
chain.say: Because your busiest tissues are quietly running on a reduced energy budget, the end result is a very specific kind of tiredness: you feel **drained even after good sleep**, because sleep restores many things but can't manually refill depleted cofactors or rebuild ATP output. During exercise your muscles hit their energy ceiling early, which shows up as **poor stamina** and that gassed-out feeling sooner than you'd expect. Afterward, **recovery is slow** because rebuilding and repairing also runs on ATP, and there simply isn't a surplus to spend. This is the tell-tale pattern — normal sleep, normal mood, normal iron and thyroid and blood sugar, yet persistent fatigue — and if it worsens on a statin, that fits perfectly, since the same medication is nudging your CoQ10 lower right where the chain began.

tell.symptoms: You've already ruled out the usual suspects — your sleep is solid, your mood is fine, and your blood tests for iron, thyroid, and blood sugar all came back normal — yet you're still running on empty, tiring fast during exercise and taking ages to bounce back afterward. If the fatigue feels worse since starting a cholesterol-lowering statin medication, that's another clue pointing here.
tell.labMarker: Standard labs are typically normal — this is largely a diagnosis of exclusion, and 'mitochondrial fatigue' in otherwise-healthy people is a plausible but under-proven label. In genuine mitochondrial disease, blood lactate may rise abnormally with modest exertion.

fixes.what: Zone-2 aerobic exercise — the strongest and best-evidenced stimulus to build new mitochondria
Creatine to buffer cellular ATP — reasonable evidence for reducing mental fatigue
CoQ10 / ubiquinol is depleted by statins and age, but trials for statin-related symptoms and fatigue are mixed-to-negative — a reasonable trial, not a proven fix
PQQ and alpha-lipoic acid are theorised to support mitochondrial function; human evidence is weak

causes.confusedWith: Simple deconditioning — real, fixable fatigue from being sedentary, which also responds to graded aerobic training; and post-viral fatigue/ME-CFS, where exertion makes things worse.
causes.keyInsight: Your cells have tiny power plants, and when they can't turn fuel into energy efficiently, you can rest all you want and still run on empty.
causes.hook: Exhausted despite good sleep, normal iron and thyroid
causes.evidence: This is a tier 2 cause: there's strong, repeated evidence linking depleted cellular cofactors and impaired energy production to fatigue and exercise intolerance, and the statin connection to lowered CoQ10 is well documented. It isn't a slam-dunk single-cause diagnosis for everyone, so treat it as a strong lead worth testing rather than a certainty.
causes.plain: Deep inside almost every cell you have hundreds of microscopic power plants called mitochondria, and their whole job is to turn the food you eat and the oxygen you breathe into usable energy. They do this on a kind of molecular assembly line, and that line needs a handful of specific helper molecules — CoQ10 and several B-vitamins — to keep running smoothly. When aging, certain medications, or ongoing cellular stress drains those helpers, the assembly line slows and each unit of fuel yields less energy. Your muscles and brain, which are the hungriest tissues you have, feel that shortfall first, which is why you can sleep well and still feel drained, gas out quickly during exercise, and recover slowly. The good news is that cofactors are replaceable, so this is one of the more addressable causes of unexplained fatigue.

chronic-fatigue.theOneThing: Fix sleep first, before reaching for any supplement. Anchor a consistent wake time, get bright light within an hour of waking, and protect a 7-9 hour sleep window. It's the single biggest lever for most people — and it also acts as a diagnostic: once your sleep is genuinely solid for two weeks, any fatigue that remains points you to a deeper driver (mood/stress, iron, thyroid, or blood sugar) worth testing.

cravings.intro: A sugar craving is almost never about weak willpower — it's a signal, and the signal has several possible sources that differ from person to person. For one person the driver is a glucose spike-and-crash that leaves the brain convinced it's running low on fuel two hours after lunch; for another it's a single short night that pushes hunger and reward signalling out of balance; for another it's cortisol from a stressful week nudging the reward system toward anything sweet; for another it's simply eating too little protein, so appetite stays switched on until the body gets what it's actually hunting for; and for people who menstruate, the week before a period reliably ratchets cravings up on its own. Most people have two or three of these stacked on top of each other. The job here is to find YOURS — the pattern below that matches when, and why, your cravings actually hit.
ladder: You crave sugar or can't stop snacking a few hours after eating
Your brain is receiving a false 'need fuel' or 'need reward' signal
Driven by glucose crashes, high ghrelin and reward drive, cortisol, unmet protein, or cyclical hormones
Rooted in how you build meals, how you sleep, your stress load, and (cyclically) your hormones — mostly modifiable
cravings.theOneThing: Rebuild your first two meals of the day around 30–40g of protein plus real fiber, eaten before any refined carbs. This single change flattens the glucose spike-and-crash that drives most afternoon cravings, satisfies the protein appetite that keeps hunger switched on, and gives a stable base so that sleep, stress and cyclical drivers have far less to pull on.

causes.name: Blood-sugar swings (reactive glucose dips)

chain.node: High-glycemic, low-fiber, low-protein meal
chain.lay: You eat something fast and carby with little to slow it down
chain.say: It starts with a particular kind of meal: something high in **fast carbs** — that means sugars and refined starches like white bread, cereal, pastries, sweet drinks, or fruit juice, which your gut breaks down into blood sugar very quickly. Crucially, this meal is also low in **fibre** (the tough, indigestible part of plants that acts like a speed bump, slowing how fast sugar enters your blood) and low in **protein** (the nutrient in eggs, meat, and beans that your body digests slowly and uses to build tissue). Because there's almost nothing in the meal to slow digestion down, all that sugar is free to flood into your bloodstream at once. Think of it as pouring sugar straight into your system with no brakes — that's the setup for everything that follows.

chain.node: Rapid glucose spike drives a large insulin response
chain.lay: Blood sugar spikes fast, so your body floods you with insulin to clear it
chain.say: Because that sugar rushed in with no brakes, your **blood sugar** — the amount of glucose, the simple sugar your cells burn for energy, floating in your blood — shoots up sharply. Your body doesn't like blood sugar staying high, so a small organ behind your stomach called the **pancreas** (a **gland**, meaning an organ whose job is to release chemical messengers) responds by releasing a big dose of **insulin**. Insulin is a **hormone** — a chemical messenger that travels through your blood carrying instructions — and its instruction is simple: it tells your cells to pull sugar out of the blood and store it away. The faster and higher your blood sugar spikes, the larger the insulin response your body fires back to match it, so a big spike means a big flood of insulin.

chain.node: Glucose overshoots downward, dipping toward or below fasting baseline ~2–3h later (reactive dip)
chain.lay: The insulin overshoots, and 2 to 3 hours later your blood sugar dips too low
chain.say: Here's the catch: because that insulin flood was so large, it does its job a little too well. Insulin keeps clearing sugar out of your blood even after the meal's sugar is used up, so instead of levelling off gently, your blood sugar overshoots on the way down and keeps falling. About 2 to 3 hours after the meal it can dip toward, or even below, your **fasting baseline** — the normal, steady level your blood sugar sits at when you haven't eaten for a while. This is the **reactive dip**: "reactive" because it's your body over-reacting to the earlier spike, not a sign anything is broken. A smaller, gentler rise wouldn't trigger this — it's specifically the big spike-and-flood that sets up the deep dip.

chain.node: Adrenaline (with glucagon) counter-regulation plus hypothalamic hunger drive fire — adrenaline is the main symptom driver
chain.lay: Your body reads the low as an emergency and fires adrenaline to pull sugar back up
chain.say: Your brain runs almost entirely on blood sugar, so when that sugar dips too low, your body treats it as a genuine emergency and moves fast to reverse it. It releases **adrenaline** — the "fight-or-flight" hormone, the same one you feel when you're startled — along with **glucagon**, another hormone from the pancreas that acts as insulin's opposite by telling your **liver** (the large organ that, among many other jobs, keeps a reserve of sugar tucked away) to release that stored sugar back into the blood. At the same time, a control centre deep in your brain called the **hypothalamus** (the region that manages hunger, thirst, and body temperature) switches on a powerful drive to eat. Adrenaline is the main thing you actually feel here: it's built to jolt you into action, so it's what makes your hands shaky, your mood short, and your focus scatter. In other words, the unpleasant symptoms aren't the low sugar itself — they're your body's alarm bells ringing to fix it.

chain.node: Craving for fast carbs, shakiness, energy slump, irritability
chain.lay: The result: shaky, irritable, slumping, and craving fast carbs
chain.say: Because adrenaline is flooding your system and your hypothalamus is screaming for fuel, all of that lands as a cluster of very physical feelings. You get **shakiness** and a jittery, on-edge mood from the adrenaline, an **energy slump** as your brain runs short on its usual sugar supply, and **irritability** because a stressed, under-fuelled brain has a short fuse. On top of that comes a laser-focused **craving for fast carbs** — not just any food, but specifically the quick sugar your body knows will lift blood sugar the fastest. That's why a few bites of something sweet fix it within minutes, and also why the whole cycle can restart: reach for another fast-carb snack and you set up the very next spike, dip, and craving all over again.

tell.symptoms: Cravings and hunger hit you roughly 2 to 4 hours after a carb-heavy meal — often alongside shakiness, irritability, or a sudden energy slump — and a few bites of something sweet fix it within minutes. It's noticeably worse after a breakfast of cereal, toast, or juice than after something like eggs.
tell.labMarker: CGM shows glucose rising sharply then dipping toward/below fasting baseline within ~2–3h; large 'dippers' report more hunger and eat more (PREDICT study — though the correlations are modest, r≈0.16–0.27). Importantly these dips occur even in metabolically healthy people, so a dip alone does NOT prove insulin resistance; check fasting insulin/HOMA-IR only to see whether IR is also present.

fixes.what: Build meals protein-and-fiber-first, carbs last, to blunt the spike and the rebound dip
Front-load 25–40g protein and 8–12g fiber per meal
Berberine to improve insulin sensitivity and flatten post-meal glucose (only useful if IR is present)
Myo-inositol to improve insulin signaling (best evidence in PCOS/insulin resistance)
Magnesium — repletion supports insulin sensitivity if you're deficient
Semaglutide (GLP-1 agonist) for refractory cravings with insulin resistance/obesity — slows gastric emptying, blunts glucose swings and appetite

causes.confusedWith: True organic hypoglycemia (rare — e.g. insulinoma) or early/undiagnosed diabetes; get glucose formally checked if dips are severe, occur fasted, or don't track meals.
causes.keyInsight: A fast carby meal doesn't just raise your blood sugar — it can overshoot on the way back down, dropping you below where you started, and it's that dip a couple of hours later, not the meal itself, that makes you crave sugar.
causes.hook: Carby breakfast, then shaky and starving two hours later?
causes.evidence: This is a well-established, textbook mechanism (Tier 3): the rise-and-dip pattern of blood sugar and the adrenaline surge that follows are directly measurable in the lab. What varies is the size of the effect — some people dip far more sharply than others on the identical meal, so how strongly you feel it is genuinely individual.
causes.plain: When you eat fast-digesting carbs with little fibre or protein to slow them down, your blood sugar shoots up quickly, and your body answers with a large surge of insulin — the hormone that clears sugar out of your blood. Because that surge is large, it can pull your blood sugar down too far, so about 2 to 3 hours later you dip toward or even below where you started before eating. Your body treats that low as a mild emergency and releases adrenaline (the "fight-or-flight" chemical) to push sugar back up, and adrenaline is what actually makes you feel shaky, irritable, and desperate for fast carbs. This is why a fibre-and-protein breakfast like eggs keeps you steady while cereal and juice leave you hunting for a snack mid-morning — the smoother the rise, the smaller the crash.

causes.name: Sleep debt (ghrelin up, reward drive up)

chain.node: Short or fragmented sleep (<7h)
chain.lay: You slept too little, or your sleep kept breaking up
chain.say: This whole chain starts with **sleep debt** — the shortfall your body carries when you regularly get less than about **seven hours** of sleep, or when your sleep is **fragmented**, meaning it keeps breaking into pieces because you wake up repeatedly through the night. Your body treats sleep as essential maintenance time, and when it doesn't get enough, it doesn't just leave you feeling tired — it quietly shifts several of your internal chemical signals to cope. The signals it changes most predictably are the ones that control hunger and food reward, which is exactly why appetite is one of the first things to go haywire after a bad night. So the trigger here isn't a food, a craving, or a lack of willpower — it's simply the amount and quality of sleep you got. Everything that follows is your body reacting to that shortfall.

chain.node: Ghrelin (hunger hormone) rises — the most reliable hormonal change
chain.lay: Your hunger hormone climbs
chain.say: Because your body registered that sleep shortfall, it raises a **hormone** called **ghrelin**. A **hormone** is just a chemical messenger your body releases into your blood to tell distant organs what to do, and **ghrelin** is the specific messenger that means "you're hungry, go find food" — it's made mostly by the lining of your **stomach** and rises naturally before meals to prompt you to eat. Out of all the chemical changes that happen after poor sleep, this rise in **ghrelin** is the most reliable and best-documented one. So the morning after a short or broken night, your stomach is essentially sending a stronger, more frequent "I'm hungry" signal to your brain than it normally would — even if you've eaten a perfectly adequate amount. That extra push is the first reason the cravings feel harder to argue with than usual.

chain.node: Endocannabinoid tone (2-AG) and mesolimbic reward sensitivity to calorie-dense food rise
chain.lay: Your brain's reward system turns up the volume on rich food
chain.say: On top of that hunger signal, poor sleep raises something called your **endocannabinoid tone**, and in particular a molecule named **2-AG**. **Endocannabinoids** are chemicals your own body makes that plug into the same "feel-good" docking points as the active ingredient in cannabis — which is exactly why cannabis famously triggers "the munchies" — and raising their **tone** simply means there's more of this appetite-and-pleasure signalling switched on. At the same time, your **mesolimbic reward system** — the deep brain circuit that runs on a chemical called **dopamine** and decides how good and how tempting something feels — becomes more sensitive to **calorie-dense food**, meaning food that's high in sugar or fat. So because your sleep-deprived brain has both more of these pleasure chemicals active and a more responsive reward circuit, a doughnut or a bowl of pasta genuinely lights up your brain more strongly than it would on a well-rested day. It's not that you've become greedy — the same food is simply registering as a bigger reward.

chain.node: Heightened sweet-taste preference plus more waking hours to eat
chain.lay: Sweet things taste better, and you're awake longer to reach for them
chain.say: With your reward system running hot, two very practical things shift. First, your **sweet-taste preference** rises — this is just the strength of your liking for sweetness, and after poor sleep your tongue and brain rate sweet flavours as more pleasant and more wanted than usual, which steers you specifically toward sugary and starchy foods rather than, say, a salad. Second, because you slept less, you are simply **awake for more hours**, and every extra waking hour is another window in which hunger can build and another opportunity to eat — especially the late-evening and night hours when you'd normally have been asleep. So the heightened craving from the previous step now has both a clear target (sweet, comforting food) and extra time to act on it. Put plainly: the food you're drawn to has narrowed to the sweet and starchy end, and the clock has handed you more chances to give in.

chain.node: Increased hunger, sweet/starchy comfort-food cravings, evening/late-night snacking
chain.lay: You end up hungrier, craving comfort food, snacking late
chain.say: All of that lands as the symptom you actually notice: you feel **hungrier** overall, and your cravings point firmly at **sweet and starchy comfort food** — chocolate, biscuits, bread, pasta, and similar — rather than at balanced meals. Because the effect is strongest in your extra waking hours, it tends to peak in the **evening and late at night**, which is why post-bad-sleep days so often end with snacking you didn't plan. This is the combined result of everything upstream: more **ghrelin** shouting hunger, a **reward system** making rich food feel better, and a stronger pull toward sweetness with more hours to indulge it. The genuinely hopeful part is that none of these changes are permanent — they're your body's temporary response to lost sleep, so when you string together a few solid nights, the whole chain quietens and the cravings largely disappear on their own.

tell.symptoms: Your cravings are clearly worse the day after a short or broken night's sleep, and they lean toward sweets and starchy comfort foods — bread, pasta, biscuits, chocolate. They tend to hit hardest in the evening or late at night, and on days when you've slept well they mostly fade on their own.
tell.labMarker: No reliable clinical lab. Acute sleep-restriction crossover RCTs reliably raise ghrelin, sweet-taste preference and food intake; leptin changes are inconsistent across studies, so the popular 'leptin crashes when you're tired' story is oversimplified. Confirm by tracking sleep duration against next-day craving intensity.

fixes.what: Protect a consistent 7–9h sleep window with a fixed wake time
Cut caffeine after early afternoon and dim bright/blue light in the evening
Protein-rich breakfast to counter the next-day hunger/reward shift
Magnesium in the evening may support sleep quality if you're deficient

causes.keyInsight: A short or broken night doesn't just make you tired — it turns up your hunger hormone and makes sugary, starchy food feel more rewarding, so the "willpower problem" you feel the next day is really a chemistry problem you can fix by sleeping.
causes.hook: The day after bad sleep, sugar feels impossible to resist.
causes.evidence: This is a well-established mechanism: controlled sleep studies reliably show that cutting sleep raises the hunger hormone ghrelin and increases the brain's reward response to calorie-dense food. The exact size of the effect varies from person to person, but the direction — less sleep, more sweet-craving — is consistent and well documented.
causes.plain: When you sleep less than about seven hours, or your sleep is broken into fragments, your body raises a hunger hormone called ghrelin — the single most reliable hormonal change seen after poor sleep. At the same time, your brain's internal "pleasure" chemistry ramps up and calorie-dense foods start to feel more rewarding than usual, while your tongue's preference for sweetness increases. Being awake longer also simply gives you more hours in which to eat. Together these push you toward sweet and starchy comfort food, especially in the evening. The reassuring part: because this is driven by sleep, a few good nights usually turns the cravings back down without any special dieting.

causes.name: Chronic stress (cortisol-driven hedonic eating)

chain.node: Chronic psychological stress / poor stress recovery
chain.lay: Stress that never really switches off
chain.say: This whole chain starts with **chronic stress** — meaning stress that keeps going day after day instead of ending and letting you recover. Your body is built to handle short bursts of pressure and then return to calm, a return called **stress recovery**. When the pressure is constant — a demanding job, worry, poor sleep, no real downtime — you never fully get that calm reset. So instead of your stress system firing briefly and standing down, it stays switched on in the background. That unresolved, always-on state is the trigger that sets everything below in motion.

chain.node: Sustained cortisol elevation signals a need for quick energy and raises reward sensitivity
chain.lay: Your stress hormone stays high and starts craving quick energy
chain.say: Because your stress system is stuck in the on position, your body keeps releasing **cortisol** — a **hormone** (a chemical messenger that travels in your blood to tell distant parts of your body what to do) that is your main stress signal. Cortisol's ancient job is to get you ready for a threat, and one way it does that is by demanding **quick energy** — fast fuel your body can burn right away, which in food terms means sugar and fat. On top of that, cortisol turns up your **reward sensitivity**, meaning it makes pleasurable things feel more tempting than usual. So because cortisol stays high, your body is now both asking for fast fuel and primed to find sugary, fatty foods extra appealing. This sets the stage for where those cravings actually get generated.

chain.node: Reward system shifts toward sugar/fat (nucleus accumbens, opioid system) while prefrontal self-control is impaired
chain.lay: Your brain's pleasure system leans toward sugar while your self-control weakens
chain.say: Now that cortisol has raised your reward sensitivity, that heightened craving lands in your brain's **reward system** — the network that makes certain things feel good so you'll do them again. A key hub here is the **nucleus accumbens**, a small brain area that flags experiences as pleasurable, and it works closely with your **opioid system**, your body's own built-in feel-good chemistry that produces a soothing, comforting sensation. Under sustained stress this machinery tilts specifically toward sugar and fat, because those deliver the fast, strong reward it's now hunting for. At the same time, stress dampens your **prefrontal cortex** — the front part of your brain that handles planning and self-control, the part that would normally say 'you're not actually hungry, skip it.' So you get a double hit: the craving is amplified and the brake that would stop you is weakened, which is why willpower alone feels so useless here.

chain.node: Comfort-eating; palatable food transiently blunts glucocorticoid stress reactivity, reinforcing the loop
chain.lay: Comfort-eating briefly calms your stress — and that teaches your brain to repeat it
chain.say: With the craving turned up and self-control turned down, you do the natural thing — you eat something sweet or rich, which is what **comfort-eating** means: eating to feel better rather than to fill an empty stomach. Here's the important part: **palatable food** (simply food that tastes really good, especially sugary and fatty food) does something real to your body — it briefly **blunts your glucocorticoid stress reactivity**. That phrase sounds technical, but **glucocorticoid** just means the family of stress hormones that cortisol belongs to, so this is only saying the food temporarily quiets down that same cortisol-driven stress response we've been tracking. So for a short while you genuinely feel calmer and more soothed. Your brain notices that the food solved the discomfort, and it files that away as a lesson: 'stress hurts, food fixes it.' That learned lesson is what closes the **loop** — it wires the comfort into a habit, so the next stressful moment pulls you straight back to sugar.

chain.node: Emotional, non-hunger sugar cravings, especially after stressful days
chain.lay: You end up with emotional cravings that spike after stressful days
chain.say: Because that loop has now been reinforced again and again, your cravings stop lining up with real physical hunger and start lining up with your **emotions** instead. This is why they feel **non-hunger** — they show up when you're tense, drained, or upset, not when your stomach is genuinely empty and your body needs fuel. They're strongest in the **evening after a demanding day**, because that's when the day's stress has piled up and your self-control is most worn down. So the pattern you actually experience — reaching for specific comfort foods when you're stressed rather than hungry — isn't a character flaw. It's the visible end-point of a stress hormone, a reward system, and a learned habit all pulling in the same direction.

tell.symptoms: Your cravings seem to follow your emotions and stress levels rather than real physical hunger — you reach for specific "comfort" foods when you're tense, tired, or wound-up, not when your stomach is actually empty. They tend to spike in the evening after a demanding day.
tell.labMarker: No routine diagnostic test; an elevated or flattened late-evening salivary cortisol can corroborate high stress load, but this cause is identified behaviorally, not by a lab.

fixes.what: Insert a non-food stress down-regulation habit at the usual craving time (walk, breathwork, short exercise)
Keep protein + fiber at each meal so stress cravings don't land on an empty, glucose-swinging tank
Prioritize sleep — sleep debt and cortisol amplify each other
Magnesium — depleted by chronic stress; may support HPA-axis regulation if deficient

causes.confusedWith: Binge-eating disorder — if eating feels out of control with distress/secrecy, that needs targeted support beyond a craving protocol.
causes.keyInsight: When life stays stressful, your body keeps pumping out a survival hormone that literally rewires your brain to want sugar — so the craving isn't weakness, it's your stress system asking for fast fuel and comfort.
causes.hook: Cravings that track your mood, not your empty stomach
causes.evidence: This is a strong, well-documented association: studies consistently link ongoing stress and higher stress-hormone levels to more comfort-eating and sugar cravings. The exact wiring in humans is still being mapped, so we treat it as a robust pattern rather than a fully nailed-down mechanism.
causes.plain: When stress never fully switches off, your body keeps releasing cortisol, its main stress hormone. Cortisol's job is to prepare you for a challenge by freeing up quick energy — and part of how it does that is by making sweet, fatty foods feel more rewarding. At the same time, ongoing stress dampens the thinking part of your brain that would normally say "not now," so the pull toward sugar wins more easily. Eating those foods briefly quiets your stress response, which feels like relief — and that relief teaches your brain to do it again next time you're stressed. The result is a loop: emotional, non-hunger cravings that show up most strongly after hard days.

causes.name: Under-eating protein (protein leverage)

chain.node: Low-protein diet diluted by processed carbs and fats
chain.lay: Your meals are low in protein and padded out with carbs and fats
chain.say: **Protein** is the building-block nutrient in foods like meat, eggs, fish, dairy and beans — your body uses it to build and repair muscle, skin, hormones and almost everything else, so it needs a certain amount every day. The trouble starts when your meals are **low in protein** and instead **diluted** — watered down — by lots of **processed carbs and fats**, meaning foods like bread, pastries, chips, sugary snacks and oily sauces. **Carbs**, short for **carbohydrates**, are simply the sugars and starches in food that give it quick energy, and these particular foods are low in protein but heavy in those energy-dense carbs and fats. Think of a skipped or bread-only breakfast, or a day with very little meat or dairy: each mouthful is delivering plenty of energy but very little of the one nutrient your body is actually keeping score of. So even after a big-feeling meal, the specific thing your body wanted — protein — is still running short, and that quiet shortfall is what sets the whole craving cycle in motion in the steps that follow.

chain.node: Protein target unmet → FGF21, the leading candidate 'low-protein' signal, rises and drives protein appetite (strong in rodents; emerging human data)
chain.lay: A 'not enough protein yet' signal switches on and turns up your appetite
chain.say: Because that protein shortfall from the last step goes unmet, your body sends up a chemical alarm. The main suspect is a **hormone** called **FGF21** — a hormone simply being a chemical messenger that your organs release into your blood to tell other parts of the body what to do. When your protein intake stays too low, levels of **FGF21** rise, and it acts on your brain to crank up **protein appetite**, which is a specific hunger aimed at protein-rich food rather than food in general. In other words, this isn't random hunger — it's your body actively driving you to go find and eat more protein because it senses it hasn't had enough. This link is very strong in animal studies and is still emerging in humans, but it lines up neatly with the everyday experience that low-protein days leave you restless and searching for something more.

chain.node: Drive to keep eating until protein need is met, overshooting on carbs/calories
chain.lay: You keep eating to hit your protein quota — and overshoot on carbs and calories
chain.say: Now that this protein-seeking drive from the last step is switched on, it won't fully quiet down until your protein need is actually met — and that's where the overeating creeps in. Because your meals are still **diluted** with carbs and fats, each portion carries only a little protein, so to reach your daily **protein target** you have to keep eating far more total food. Picture needing a fixed number of protein 'bricks' but only getting a couple per plate — you end up going back again and again, and every extra plate also piles on carbs and calories you never needed. This is the heart of **protein leverage**: your appetite 'leverages', or pushes up, your whole food intake just to chase that one missing nutrient. So the very thing meant to protect you — a drive to get enough protein — ends up making you eat more of everything else.

chain.node: Persistent grazing, never feeling truly satisfied, snack cravings
chain.lay: You graze all day, never feel truly full, and keep reaching for snacks
chain.say: Because that protein target from the last step is so hard to hit on a low-protein day, the appetite signal barely ever switches off — and that's exactly what you feel as the symptom. You end up **grazing**, meaning nibbling and snacking on and off all day rather than feeling done after a proper meal, and you're never quite **satisfied** — that comfortable 'I've had enough now' feeling stays just out of reach. Snack cravings keep returning, usually pulling you toward quick carby foods that, frustratingly, don't carry the protein your body is still waiting for, so the loop simply repeats. The tell-tale sign that this is what's happening to you is the contrast: on days when you eat more protein, the grazing and cravings noticeably settle, because your body finally gets the signal it was chasing and lets your appetite stand down.

tell.symptoms: You feel hungry again soon after eating, graze all day, and never feel truly 'full' — and it tends to happen on days built around carby, low-meat, low-dairy meals like a skipped or bread-only breakfast. On higher-protein days, the cravings noticeably ease.
tell.labMarker: No lab; estimate dietary protein — chronic intake below ~1.2 g/kg body weight (or <25–30g per meal) makes protein leverage plausible. Protein leverage is a well-supported hypothesis, not settled fact.

fixes.what: Hit 25–40g of protein at each main meal, starting with breakfast
Eat protein first in the meal to reach satiety before excess carbs

causes.keyInsight: Your body counts protein, not calories — if a meal is short on protein, your appetite keeps you eating (usually more carbs) until the protein quota is finally hit.
causes.hook: Never full, grazing all day on low-protein meals?
causes.evidence: Tier 2 — strong association. The idea that appetite tracks a protein target (called 'protein leverage') is well supported by human feeding studies, and FGF21 is the leading candidate signal behind it — proven powerfully in animals, with human evidence still emerging rather than fully settled.
causes.plain: Your appetite doesn't just chase calories — it chases a set amount of protein each day, a pattern researchers call 'protein leverage'. When your meals are watered down with processed carbs and fats, each bite carries less protein, so you have to eat more total food to hit that protein target — and the extra food is mostly carbs and calories you didn't actually need. A hormone called FGF21 appears to be the messenger that flags 'not enough protein yet' and keeps your appetite switched on. The result is grazing, weak fullness, and snack cravings that quietly ease the moment you eat enough protein.

causes.name: Hyperpalatable food & reward conditioning (hedonic hunger)

chain.node: Frequent ultra-processed foods engineered with sugar+fat+salt
chain.lay: Foods built to be almost impossible to resist
chain.say: The starting point is what you're eating. **Ultra-processed foods** — packaged, industrially made products like sweets, chips, biscuits, sodas and fast food — are deliberately engineered to combine **sugar**, **fat** and **salt** in ratios that barely exist in natural whole foods. This matters because your taste and reward systems evolved in a world where such an intense combination was rare and precious, so they treat it as an unusually big prize. When you eat these foods often, you are repeatedly exposing your brain to a reward hit far stronger than anything a plain apple or piece of chicken delivers. Nothing has gone wrong yet — you've simply given your reward system a very loud, very frequent signal to respond to.

chain.node: Mesolimbic dopamine reward surge on eating them
chain.lay: Your brain's 'reward' chemical spikes unusually hard
chain.say: Because that sugar-fat-salt combination is so intense, eating it triggers a bigger-than-normal burst of a brain chemical called **dopamine**. Dopamine is a **neurotransmitter** — a messenger molecule that brain cells use to signal each other — and its job here is to flag "that was rewarding, pay attention, do it again." This surge happens along the **mesolimbic pathway**, which is simply the specific brain circuit that handles reward and motivation (you can think of it as the brain's reward highway). Whole, unprocessed foods produce a modest dopamine response, but these engineered foods produce a much larger one, precisely because they were designed to. So every time you eat them, your reward highway lights up far brighter than it would for ordinary food — and your brain takes note of exactly what and when that happened.

chain.node: Cue-conditioned 'wanting' (incentive sensitization) builds while normal satiety signalling is blunted
chain.lay: Your brain learns to crave the cue, and 'full' stops registering
chain.say: When that strong dopamine signal fires over and over, your brain does what it's built to do — it learns. It starts tying the reward to the **cues** that surround it — the sight of the packet, the walk past the shop, a particular time of day, a certain mood — so that those cues alone begin to trigger a pull toward the food. Scientists call this **incentive sensitization**: the "**wanting**" (the urge to seek and get the food) grows stronger and more automatic over time, and it becomes separate from actually "liking" the food when you eat it. While this wanting is being cranked up, the opposite system gets turned down: your **satiety signalling** becomes blunted. Satiety signalling means the fullness messages carried by **hormones** — chemical messengers your body releases into your bloodstream to carry instructions between organs, such as **leptin** — that normally tell your brain "you've had enough, stop now," and once they are blunted they become far less effective at applying the brakes. So one side of the scale is pulling harder toward the food while the other side has gone quiet.

chain.node: Cravings triggered by cues/habit rather than hunger; escalation and 'can't stop once I start'
chain.lay: You crave on autopilot instead of on real hunger
chain.say: With wanting now wired to cues and the fullness brakes weakened, cravings stop lining up with genuine hunger. Because your brain has learned the cues, the urge switches on when you see a screen, pass the vending machine, or hit that familiar time of day — even when your stomach is full and your body needs no energy. And because the satiety signals that would normally say "enough" are muted, once you start eating there's little internal stop signal, which is the "can't stop once I start" feeling. Over time the same system that made the food rewarding also nudges the amount upward, so the portion it takes to feel satisfied slowly climbs — the "tolerance" creep you may have noticed. The whole loop now runs on cue and habit rather than on real hunger, which is exactly why willpower in the moment feels so hard: you're fighting a response your brain has spent months or years learning.

tell.symptoms: You find yourself craving specific treat foods even when you're physically full, and the urge often gets switched on by a cue — a screen lighting up, walking past the vending machine, or just hitting a certain time of day — rather than by real hunger. Once you start it's hard to stop, and the portion that satisfies you has slowly crept upward over time.
tell.labMarker: No lab; identified by the cue-driven, reward-seeking pattern rather than a metabolic marker.

fixes.what: Design the environment — remove the trigger foods and cues from home/desk so the conditioned response isn't fired
Reduce ultra-processed food exposure for a few weeks to let reward sensitivity re-normalize
Swap to whole-food versions that satisfy without the engineered reward spike
Semaglutide (GLP-1 agonist) reduces reward-driven 'wanting'/food noise and hedonic eating in resistant cases (reduces 'wanting' more than 'liking')

causes.keyInsight: Your cravings aren't a hunger problem — they're a learning problem. Foods engineered to be extra-rewarding train your brain to want them on cue, while quietly turning down the signal that tells you you're full.
causes.hook: You crave junk when full — cues, not hunger, drive it
causes.evidence: This is a tier 2 link — a strong, well-replicated association. Human brain-imaging and animal studies consistently show that highly processed, sugar-fat-salt foods drive exaggerated reward responses and cue-triggered wanting, though exactly how much this alone explains any one person's eating is still being mapped.
causes.plain: "Hedonic hunger" means eating driven by pleasure and reward rather than by your body's actual need for energy. Modern ultra-processed foods are formulated to hit combinations of sugar, fat and salt that almost never occur in nature, which produces an unusually strong reward signal in the brain each time you eat them. With repetition, your brain starts to link everyday cues — sights, smells, times, places — to the expectation of that reward, so the craving fires from the cue itself. At the same time the normal "I'm full now" signalling becomes less effective at putting the brakes on, so you keep going. The result is craving and overeating that runs on habit and environment, not on genuine hunger.

causes.name: Luteal-phase / premenstrual cravings (cyclical)

chain.node: Late luteal (premenstrual) phase — for people who menstruate
chain.lay: You've entered the week before your period
chain.say: Your body runs on a roughly monthly **menstrual cycle** — the repeating pattern that prepares your body for a possible pregnancy and, if none happens, ends with your period (the days of bleeding). The stretch of that cycle just after you release an egg and before your next period is called the **luteal phase**, and this craving pattern kicks in during the **late luteal phase** — the last several days of that stretch, the roughly 7 to 10 days before bleeding starts. Nothing has gone wrong here; this is simply a normal, recurring point in the cycle for anyone who menstruates. The reason it matters is that this specific window is when the hormone changes behind your cravings begin — so think of it as the starting gun for everything that follows.

chain.node: Progesterone high and estrogen falling; central serotonin availability drops
chain.lay: Your mood-steadying brain chemical dips as your hormones shift
chain.say: Because you've reached this late luteal window, your **hormones** — the body's chemical messengers that travel in your blood and tell different organs what to do — start changing levels. Specifically, a hormone called **progesterone** (which helps prepare the body to support a pregnancy) is running high, while **estrogen** (the main hormone that drives much of the cycle) is now falling. This matters because estrogen normally helps your brain keep up its supply of **serotonin** — a signalling chemical your brain uses to keep your mood calm and steady. So as estrogen drops, your brain's serotonin availability drops with it, which is a big part of why the low mood and irritability tend to show up alongside the cravings.

chain.node: Carbohydrate transiently raises serotonin (mood self-medication) while resting metabolic rate and genuine energy needs rise
chain.lay: Your brain reaches for carbs to lift that chemical back up — and you need a bit more energy too
chain.say: Now that your serotonin has dipped, your brain looks for a quick way to raise it again — and eating **carbohydrates** (the sugars and starches found in sweets, bread, rice and chocolate) is exactly such a way. Here's the plain reason it works: when you eat carbs, your body releases **insulin** (a hormone that moves sugar out of your blood), and a side effect of that is helping a building block called **tryptophan** reach your brain, where it gets turned into fresh serotonin. So a carb-heavy snack genuinely nudges your mood-chemical back up for a while — a kind of accidental self-medication. On top of that, your **resting metabolic rate** — the amount of energy your body burns just to keep you alive at rest — rises slightly in this phase, so your real energy needs go up at the same time, adding a true hunger signal to the mood-driven one.

chain.node: Predictable, cyclical cravings for carbs/sweets and chocolate in the ~week before menses
chain.lay: Cravings for sweets and chocolate arrive like clockwork before your period
chain.say: Put those two pulls together — a brain trying to rebuild its serotonin and a body that genuinely needs a little more fuel — and the outcome is a predictable, **cyclical** craving that returns at the same point every month. Because the strongest lever for lifting serotonin is carbohydrate, the cravings zero in on carbs, sweets and chocolate rather than food in general. They feel intense not because you lack discipline but because two separate systems, mood and energy, are nudging you the same direction at once. And because the whole thing is driven by the hormone dip of the late luteal phase, the cravings reliably ease once your period starts and your hormones begin their next cycle.

tell.symptoms: Your cravings show up like clockwork in the roughly 7 to 10 days before your period, tend to ease once bleeding starts, and often arrive alongside low mood, irritability or tiredness. They're strongest for carbs, sweets and chocolate rather than food in general.
tell.labMarker: No lab needed; diagnosed by cycle-tracking — cravings clustering in the late-luteal (premenstrual) week. Measured carbohydrate intake is highest in the luteal phase across studies, and eating somewhat more then is physiologically appropriate (RMR is elevated).

fixes.what: Track your cycle so luteal cravings are expected, not treated as a willpower 'failure'
Anchor the luteal week on steady complex carbs + protein to raise serotonin without a spike-and-crash
Don't over-restrict calories in the luteal week — energy needs are genuinely higher
Magnesium — limited evidence for reducing PMS symptoms/cravings, reasonable to trial

causes.confusedWith: PMDD (premenstrual dysphoric disorder) — if mood symptoms are severe or disabling, that's a distinct condition needing clinical treatment, not just a craving protocol.
causes.keyInsight: Your period-week sweet tooth isn't weak willpower — it's your brain reaching for carbs to top up a feel-good chemical that naturally dips when your hormones shift before your period.
causes.hook: Carb and chocolate cravings that show up every month before your period
causes.evidence: This is a tier 2 cause — a strong, well-documented association. The link between the premenstrual phase and increased carbohydrate cravings is consistently seen and biologically plausible, though the exact size of each hormone's contribution still varies from person to person.
causes.plain: In the week or so before your period, your reproductive hormones shift: progesterone rises while estrogen falls. Because estrogen normally helps keep up your brain's supply of serotonin — a chemical that steadies your mood — that falling estrogen lets serotonin dip too. Eating carbohydrates gives your brain a short-lived way to nudge serotonin back up, so your body starts steering you toward sweet and starchy foods to feel better. At the same time, your body is genuinely burning a little more energy in this phase, which adds a real hunger signal on top of the mood one. The result is a predictable, cyclical pull toward carbs, sweets and chocolate that fades once your period begins.

ladder: Cold, tired, gaining weight, dry skin, foggy
Too little active thyroid hormone (T3) is reaching your cells
Either the gland can't make enough T4, or your body can't convert T4 into active T3
Missing raw materials (iodine, selenium, iron), an autoimmune attack on the gland, or too little food and too much stress
thyroid.intro: Feeling cold, slow, and puffy when everyone else feels fine is what it looks like when your cells stop getting enough active thyroid hormone (T3) — the master dial on your metabolic rate. But "slow thyroid" is not one disease with one fix. In one person the gland itself is under quiet autoimmune attack; in another the gland is fine but starved of the iodine it needs as raw material; in a third the gland makes plenty of the storage hormone T4 but the body can't convert it to active T3 because selenium, iron, or calories are missing. These produce nearly identical symptoms yet need opposite fixes — and a "normal TSH" can hide several of them. The job below is to help you find YOUR driver, because chasing the wrong one wastes months.

causes.name: Hashimoto's autoimmune thyroiditis

chain.node: Genetic + environmental autoimmune trigger
chain.lay: Your body flips an internal switch and starts treating your own thyroid as an enemy
chain.say: Your **immune system** is the body's defence force — the network of cells that hunts down and destroys germs like bacteria and viruses. Normally it has a careful sense of "self" and leaves your own organs alone. In Hashimoto's, a mix of **genes** (the instructions you inherit from your parents) and **environmental triggers** (things like a viral infection, stress, pregnancy, or a flood of **iodine** — a mineral in salt and seafood that the thyroid uses as its raw material) tips that system out of balance, so it mistakenly tags your **thyroid** as a threat. The thyroid is a small, butterfly-shaped **gland** — an organ that makes and releases chemicals — sitting at the front of your neck, and its whole job is to set your body's speed. This first wrong decision is the seed of everything that follows: once the immune system decides the thyroid is an enemy, it will act on that belief.

chain.node: Anti-TPO / anti-thyroglobulin antibodies + cytotoxic T-cell attack
chain.lay: Your immune system builds guided weapons aimed straight at the thyroid
chain.say: Because your immune system has now flagged the thyroid as an enemy, it does what it always does to enemies — it manufactures **antibodies**, which are tiny protein tags that lock onto one specific target and mark it for destruction (a **protein** is one of the basic building-block molecules your body is made from). In Hashimoto's these antibodies are aimed at two things inside the gland: **TPO**, short for thyroid peroxidase, which is an **enzyme** (a helper molecule that speeds up a chemical reaction) the thyroid needs to actually build its **hormone** — the chemical messenger it releases into your blood to set your body's pace — and **thyroglobulin**, the large storage protein the thyroid keeps its hormone supplies packed inside. At the same time, the immune system sends in **cytotoxic T-cells** — "cytotoxic" simply means cell-killing, so these are immune cells whose job is to directly puncture and kill whatever they've been told is the enemy. So this step is the attack force assembling: labelling weapons (the antibodies) and killer troops (the T-cells), all pointed at the thyroid. Nothing is broken yet, but the assault is now armed and heading for the gland.

chain.node: Lymphocytic infiltration, fibrosis and gradual destruction of thyroid follicles
chain.lay: The attack packs the gland with immune cells and scars it, slowly killing its tiny hormone-making pouches
chain.say: Now that the killer cells and antibodies have a target, they pour into the thyroid — doctors call this **lymphocytic infiltration**, where "lymphocytes" are those immune cells and "infiltration" just means they crowd in and fill up the tissue (**tissue** is simply a group of similar cells working together as part of an organ). As the fighting drags on for months and years, the constant damage triggers **fibrosis**, which is the body laying down tough, stringy scar tissue in place of the healthy gland — the same kind of scarring you'd get over a wound, except here it's forming inside the organ and choking it. The real casualties are the **follicles**: these are the thyroid's tiny fluid-filled pouches, the actual little workshops where thyroid hormone is made and stored. Because the immune attack keeps destroying these follicles one by one, the gland's ability to produce hormone steadily crumbles. This is why Hashimoto's is so quiet and gradual — you're losing follicles slowly, and the neck can even feel enlarged (a **goiter**, meaning a swollen thyroid) early on from all the swelling before it eventually shrinks as scar tissue takes over.

chain.node: Falling T4/T3 output, rising TSH as the pituitary shouts louder
chain.lay: With the factory crumbling, thyroid hormone drops and the brain shouts for more
chain.say: Because so many follicles have now been destroyed, the thyroid simply can't make as much of its two hormones — **T4** and **T3** — as it used to. A **hormone** is a chemical messenger that travels in your blood to tell distant parts of the body what to do, and T4 and T3 are the ones that tell nearly every cell how fast to run. Sitting in your brain is the **pituitary**, a pea-sized gland that acts as the thyroid's manager: it constantly checks the hormone level in your blood and, when it senses the level dropping, it sends out more of a signal called **TSH** (thyroid-stimulating hormone) to order the thyroid to work harder. So as T4 and T3 fall, your TSH climbs higher and higher — the pituitary is effectively shouting louder and louder at a factory that can no longer answer. That rising TSH alongside falling thyroid hormone is exactly the fingerprint a blood test looks for, and it's the direct chemical consequence of the follicles being destroyed in the step before.

chain.node: Cold, fatigue, weight gain, dry skin, hair thinning
chain.lay: Everything that runs on thyroid hormone slows down, and you finally feel it
chain.say: Because T4 and T3 are the messengers that set the pace of nearly every cell, having too little of them means your whole **metabolism** — the rate at which your body burns fuel to make energy and heat — winds down, and that single slowdown explains the scattered symptoms that don't seem connected. You feel **cold** and **fatigued** because your cells are literally generating less warmth and energy than before. You notice **weight gain** because a slower metabolism burns fewer calories even when nothing about your eating has changed. Your **skin turns dry** and your **hair thins** because these fast-renewing tissues depend on brisk thyroid signalling to keep replacing themselves, and that upkeep has slowed to a crawl. This is the payoff of the whole chain — a hidden immune attack, quietly destroying the thyroid over years, finally showing up as a cluster of everyday symptoms that fluctuate as the gland's remaining output rises and falls.

tell.symptoms: Do the tired, cold, foggy, weight-creeping feelings come on slowly over months or years — sometimes easing off and then flaring again rather than hitting you all at once? And has the front of your neck ever felt swollen, full, or tight, or have you noticed your skin turning dry and your hair thinning around the same time?
tell.labMarker: TPO antibodies positive (assay-dependent, typically >35 IU/mL) is the confirming test; usually with TSH high and free T4 low or low-normal.

fixes.what: Levothyroxine (T4) replacement titrated to TSH, added T3 if conversion is poor
Selenium 100-200 mcg/day modestly lowers TPO antibody titres in trials (effect real but inconsistent) and supports the gland's antioxidant defence
Screen for and treat coexisting celiac disease; trial gluten removal if antibodies stay high
Correct low vitamin D and the nutrient deficits (iron, zinc) that ride alongside autoimmune thyroiditis

causes.confusedWith: Transient/postpartum thyroiditis, which can cause the same picture but self-resolves and is antibody-variable
causes.keyInsight: Hashimoto's isn't your thyroid "wearing out" — it's your own immune system mistaking your thyroid for an invader and slowly dismantling the very factory that makes the hormone controlling your body's speed.
causes.hook: Cold, tired, gaining weight — is your immune system attacking your thyroid?
causes.evidence: This is a tier 3, firmly established mechanism — Hashimoto's is the single most common cause of an underactive thyroid in parts of the world that get enough iodine, and the specific immune attack behind it has been confirmed by decades of blood-antibody tests and studies of thyroid tissue. The science of what is happening is not in doubt.
causes.plain: Hashimoto's is an autoimmune disease, meaning your immune system — the defence force that normally attacks germs — turns by mistake against your own thyroid, the small butterfly-shaped gland in your neck that sets your body's pace. It slowly floods the gland with immune cells and scar tissue, destroying the tiny structures that make thyroid hormone. As those structures die off, hormone output falls and your metabolism — the rate at which your body burns fuel and makes heat — winds down, which is why you feel cold, tired, and slow. It usually creeps in over months to years and often runs alongside other autoimmune conditions in you or your family. It is diagnosed with a simple blood test that looks for the tell-tale antibodies and for a slowing thyroid.

causes.name: Iodine deficiency (or excess)

chain.node: Low dietary iodine (no iodized salt, little seafood/dairy/eggs) or, conversely, iodine excess
chain.lay: You're getting too little iodine from food — or, less often, way too much
chain.say: Let's start with the raw material. Your **thyroid** is a small, butterfly-shaped **gland** — a gland is just an organ whose job is to make and release chemicals — that sits low in the front of your neck. Its whole purpose is to produce **thyroid hormones**, and a hormone is simply a chemical messenger that travels in your blood to tell the rest of your body what to do. Here's the catch: your thyroid physically builds those hormones around atoms of **iodine**, a mineral your body cannot manufacture and can only get from what you eat — mainly **iodized salt, seafood, dairy and eggs**. If your diet is low in all of those, or your body's demand jumps (as it does during **pregnancy or breastfeeding**), you simply don't take in enough. The mirror-image trigger also matters: swallowing far too much iodine — think daily **kelp tablets or high-dose iodine supplements** — can backfire and temporarily shut the gland down, so both the empty end and the overloaded end of the scale start the same problem.

chain.node: Not enough iodide substrate for TPO to iodinate thyroglobulin and build hormone
chain.lay: Without enough iodine, the thyroid's hormone-building machine runs out of parts
chain.say: Now watch what that shortage does inside the gland. To assemble a hormone, your thyroid uses an **enzyme** — a protein that speeds up a specific chemical reaction — called **thyroid peroxidase**, or **TPO** for short. TPO's job is to take **iodide** (the usable, dissolved form of the iodine you ate) and attach it onto a large scaffolding **protein** — proteins are the body's molecular building blocks — named **thyroglobulin**, in a step called **iodination** (literally, sticking iodine on). Because you're now short on iodine, TPO doesn't have enough **substrate** — the raw ingredient an enzyme needs to work on — to do its job. The machinery is fully intact and willing, but you can't build a hormone out of an ingredient that isn't there, so finished hormone output falls. (In the excess case, the flood of iodine paradoxically blocks this same iodination step, which is why too much lands you in the same place.)

chain.node: Thyroid enlarges (goiter) trying to trap scarce iodine
chain.lay: The gland swells into a goiter, straining to grab every scrap of iodine
chain.say: Your body notices the hormone shortfall and reacts. A control centre in your brain releases more **TSH** — **thyroid-stimulating hormone**, the chemical signal that tells your thyroid to work harder — trying to squeeze more output from the struggling gland. But because the gland still can't finish hormones no matter how hard it's pushed, this constant nagging signal doesn't fix the shortage; it just drives the thyroid tissue to grow larger and larger in an attempt to trap every last scrap of scarce iodine. That visible or feelable swelling at the base of your neck is called a **goiter** — an enlarged thyroid. It's not a tumour and it's not the disease itself; it's the gland's honest, physical attempt to compensate for an ingredient it can't get enough of.

chain.node: Low T4/T3, sluggish metabolic rate
chain.lay: With too little hormone in your blood, your whole metabolism slows down
chain.say: Finally, the shortage reaches the rest of you. The two hormones your thyroid was trying to build are called **T4** and **T3** (the numbers just count how many iodine atoms each carries — four and three), and together they set your **metabolic rate**: the speed at which every cell in your body burns fuel to make energy and heat.
