One of the jobs that machinery runs is **mitophagy** (say it 'my-TOFF-a-jee'), which is just the cell's clean-up crew whose one task is to find worn-out **mitochondria** and safely dismantle them, like a maintenance team scrapping a broken generator so a fresh one can take its place. When NAD+ runs low, this clean-up crew loses its funding and slows down. So the falling helper molecule from the last step is precisely why the mitochondria clean-up crew, called mitophagy, becomes **impaired** and can no longer keep up.

chain.node: Accumulating dysfunctional mitochondria and 'zombie' senescent cells
chain.lay: Broken power plants pile up, and some exhausted cells refuse to die and start poisoning their neighbours
chain.say: Because the clean-up crew has stalled, the broken power plants it was supposed to remove now have nowhere to go — so **dysfunctional mitochondria** (power plants that are damaged and produce little energy) simply **accumulate** inside your cells, like broken machines left cluttering a factory floor. And here is the direct knock-on effect: a cell stuffed with failing, leaky power plants is under constant internal stress, and that stress can flip a switch inside it. Normally a cell too damaged to work properly would quietly shut itself down and be cleared away, but under this mitochondrial stress many instead get stuck in a strange in-between state — they stop doing their job yet refuse to die. These are **senescent cells**, nicknamed **'zombie' cells** because they are neither properly alive nor cleared away. The real trouble is that these zombie cells constantly leak out **inflammatory** signals — chemical distress messages that trigger swelling, irritation and immune activity — which spread damage to healthy neighbours nearby, so one worn-out cell drags down the whole tissue (a **tissue** is just a group of similar cells working together, like muscle or an organ lining).

chain.node: Fatigue, sarcopenia, reduced endurance, organ decline
chain.lay: With weak power plants and toxic zombie cells spreading, you feel tired, lose muscle and your body slowly declines
chain.say: Finally, all of this adds up to what you actually feel. Because your cells are now stuck with **dysfunctional mitochondria** that make less energy, and because the leaking **zombie cells** are inflaming the tissue around them, your body simply cannot produce and sustain power the way it once did — so you experience **fatigue**, a deep low energy that good sleep does not fully fix. In muscle, this steady loss of working cells and energy shows up as **sarcopenia**, the medical word for the age-related shrinking and weakening of your muscles, which is why you may notice **reduced endurance** and slower recovery after effort. Extend the same process across your heart, brain, liver and other **organs**, and you get gradual **organ decline** — each one quietly doing a little less than it used to. In short, the low-energy, weaker, slower-to-bounce-back feeling of ageing is the surface signal of tiny power plants failing and zombie cells accumulating deep inside you.

tell.symptoms: Is this you: persistent low energy, poor stamina during exercise, and slow recovery afterwards, even though you sleep enough and eat well? You may also notice you are gradually losing muscle and strength as the years pass, and everyday effort feels harder than it used to.
tell.labMarker: No validated routine clinical NAD+ or mitophagy blood test exists - this driver is inferred from age and symptoms, not directly measured. NAD+ precursors reliably raise blood NAD+ in trials, but human healthspan outcomes are still early (tier 1-2).

fixes.what: Exercise - the single strongest natural driver of mitochondrial biogenesis
NMN or NR to raise NAD+ levels (raises NAD+ reliably; downstream healthspan benefit unproven in humans)
Urolithin A to boost mitophagy and muscle endurance (best translational human RCT evidence of the mito compounds)
Ca-AKG or GlyNAC to support mitochondrial and redox function (GlyNAC has a small older-adult RCT; Ca-AKG human data are weaker)

causes.keyInsight: Every cell runs on tiny power plants, and they need a fuel-helper molecule called NAD+ to work — as you age, that molecule runs low, the worn-out power plants stop getting cleared away, and some cells refuse to die and start poisoning their neighbours. That is why "tired despite doing everything right" quietly becomes the story of getting older.
causes.hook: Tired and weak despite good sleep and food?
causes.evidence: This is a strong, well-documented association: falling NAD+ levels, a build-up of damaged mitochondria, and rising numbers of senescent "zombie" cells are consistently measured in ageing tissue across humans and animals. The exact size of each factor's contribution in any one person is still being worked out, so treat this as a robust pattern rather than a settled, precise formula.
causes.plain: Inside almost every cell are mitochondria — microscopic power plants that turn your food and oxygen into usable energy. To run, they lean on a helper molecule called NAD+, which acts like a rechargeable shuttle carrying energy around. With age, oxidative stress (everyday wear-and-tear damage from unstable oxygen fragments) mounts, NAD+ gets used up faster than it is remade, and your cells' clean-up crews stop clearing out broken mitochondria. Damaged mitochondria pile up, and some worn-out cells refuse to die, becoming "senescent" zombie cells that leak inflammation into the tissue around them. The visible result is the familiar face of ageing: low energy, weaker muscles, less endurance, and organs that slowly lose their edge.

longevity.theOneThing: Train for both strength and VO2max - resistance work plus zone-2/interval cardio, roughly 4x/week. It's the only single action that hits every driver at once: it lowers insulin, empties the mTOR/growth signal, builds mitochondria, clears inflammatory visceral fat, and preserves muscle - and cardiorespiratory fitness is the strongest modifiable predictor of how long you stay healthy (low vs high fitness ~5x mortality difference in the Mandsager/JAMA cohort, larger than smoking or diabetes).

inflammation.intro: Chronic inflammation is rarely one thing gone wrong — it's your innate immune system stuck in a low-grade \"on\" state, and several different inputs can hold that switch down at once. The final common pathway is almost always the same transcription factor (NF-κB) driving a trickle of IL-6, TNF-α and IL-1β into your blood, which is why the fix isn't a single pill but finding YOUR inputs. For one person the main driver is the visceral fat around the middle acting as a cytokine factory; for another it's blood-sugar spikes glycating proteins, too little muscle movement, a leaky gut leaking bacterial fragments, chronically short sleep, or simply the senescent cells that accumulate with age. Most people have two or three of these stacked. The whole game is identifying which rungs of the ladder are lit up in you — the achiness, slow recovery and raised CRP are the smoke, not the fire.
ladder: You feel achy, recover slowly from training, and run a high hs-CRP
Your innate immune cells are stuck in a low-grade activated state (NF-κB tone), leaking IL-6 and TNF-α
That state is fed by visceral fat, blood-sugar/AGE spikes, too little muscle movement, a leaky gut, poor sleep, and aging 'senescent' cells
Which trace back to what you eat, how much fat you carry around the middle, how much you move, how you sleep, and time
inflammation.theOneThing: Attack visceral fat by cutting refined sugar and ultra-processed food — it is the single move that removes the largest cytokine-producing tissue AND blunts the glucose/AGE spikes, and it does more than any supplement to lower CRP.

causes.name: Visceral adiposity (metabolic inflammation)

chain.node: Excess visceral fat / hypertrophic adipocytes
chain.lay: The fat cells deep in your belly get overstuffed and stressed
chain.say: The fat that matters most here isn't the soft layer you can pinch under your skin — it's **visceral fat**, the deeper fat packed tightly around your internal organs like your liver and intestines. Fat is stored inside tiny balloon-like cells called **adipocytes** (simply, fat-storage cells). When you take in more energy than you burn, these cells don't multiply so much as swell — each one stretches and fills up, a state doctors call **hypertrophic** (meaning enlarged or overgrown). An adipocyte stretched to its limit becomes physically stressed and starts to malfunction, releasing distress signals and even leaking its contents. So the story begins not with 'too much fat' in the abstract, but with individual belly-fat cells that are overfilled and struggling.

chain.node: M1 macrophage infiltration of adipose tissue (crown-like structures)
chain.lay: Immune cleanup cells rush into the fat and surround the dying cells
chain.say: Because those overstuffed fat cells are now stressed and leaking distress signals, your body reacts the way it does to any injury — it sends in immune cells to investigate and clean up. The cleanup cell here is the **macrophage**, a large white blood cell whose job is to patrol tissues and eat up debris, germs, and dying cells (the name literally means 'big eater'). Macrophages come in different moods: the aggressive, inflammation-driving version is called **M1**. Drawn in by the signals from the failing adipocytes, M1 macrophages **infiltrate** — meaning they move in and accumulate — inside your belly fat. Under a microscope you can actually see them ringing a dead or dying fat cell like a wreath, a pattern scientists nicknamed a **crown-like structure**. What was quiet storage tissue is now dotted with clusters of angry immune cells.

chain.node: NF-κB activation
chain.lay: Inside those immune cells, a master inflammation switch flips on
chain.say: Now that M1 macrophages have gathered in your fat and encountered all that cellular stress and leaked material, something has to tell them to actually start fighting — and that trigger is a molecular switch called **NF-κB**. NF-κB is a **transcription factor**, which just means a **protein** (one of the tiny molecular machines that do most of the work inside your cells) that sits in the cell and, when activated, turns specific **genes** — the coded instructions a cell follows to build things — on or off. Think of it as a master light switch wired to a whole room of inflammation genes: in calm conditions it stays off, but the stress signals the macrophages are picking up flip it to 'on'. Once **NF-κB is activated**, it moves into the cell's control centre and switches on exactly the set of genes that build inflammatory weapons. So the immune cells don't just sit there — this one switch commits them to actively manufacturing inflammation.

chain.node: TNF-α & IL-6 spill into circulation
chain.lay: The switched-on cells pump alarm molecules straight into your blood
chain.say: With the NF-κB switch flipped on, those genes get read out and the macrophages start mass-producing their inflammatory weapons — small signalling proteins called **cytokines**, which act as chemical messengers that immune cells use to shout instructions to one another. Two of the loudest here are **TNF-α** (tumour necrosis factor alpha) and **IL-6** (interleukin-6), both of which ramp up inflammation and tell nearby cells to join in. Because all of this is happening in belly fat, which is threaded with blood vessels, these cytokines don't stay put — they **spill into your circulation** and get carried around your whole body. So a localised commotion inside your abdomen is now broadcasting alarm signals to every organ you have, through the bloodstream.

chain.node: Systemic low-grade inflammation, raised CRP
chain.lay: Your whole body ends up simmering in low-level inflammation
chain.say: Once TNF-α and IL-6 are circulating everywhere, they keep your immune system gently switched on far from where the problem started — this is **systemic low-grade inflammation**, meaning a quiet, body-wide simmer rather than the obvious redness and swelling of a cut or infection. One thing IL-6 does is travel to your **liver** (the large organ that filters your blood and makes many of its proteins) and instruct it to produce **CRP**, short for C-reactive protein — a substance your liver releases whenever inflammation is present. Because CRP shows up in a simple blood test, a **raised CRP** becomes the visible fingerprint of everything happening upstream in your belly fat. This constant low simmer is exactly what nudges energy, recovery, and long-term metabolic health in the wrong direction — which is why shrinking visceral fat, not just body weight, is the lever that actually cools the whole chain down.

tell.symptoms: Your waist has been slowly creeping up (past about 90cm for men or 80cm for women on Asian measurements), and you tend to carry weight around your middle rather than your hips. You may feel flat energy and slow recovery even though the scale and your BMI (your weight-for-height number) look fine — the "skinny-fat" pattern.
tell.labMarker: hs-CRP elevated (>1 mg/L, worse >3); fasting insulin high or HOMA-IR >1.5; triglyceride/HDL ratio elevated

fixes.what: Sustained fat loss via calorie deficit — visceral fat is preferentially mobilised, shrinking the cytokine source
Resistance + zone-2 training to strip visceral fat and improve insulin sensitivity
Cut liquid sugar and ultra-processed carbs (lower sugar_g) to stop feeding fat storage
Omega-3 (EPA/DHA) — shifts adipose macrophages toward the resolving M2 phenotype and generates specialised pro-resolving mediators (resolvins)

causes.confusedWith: Cushing's or subclinical hypercortisolism, which also drives central fat plus inflammation — rule out if fat gain is rapid with purple striae
causes.keyInsight: Belly fat isn't just storage — it's an active organ that, when overstuffed, quietly leaks alarm signals into your blood and keeps your whole body on a low simmer of inflammation.
causes.hook: Growing waistline and low energy despite a "normal" weight?
causes.evidence: This is a Tier 3, established-mechanism cause: the chain from deep belly fat to immune activation to raised inflammation markers has been mapped in detail across human and lab studies and is widely accepted. What varies between people is how strong the effect is, not whether the mechanism is real.
causes.plain: The fat stored deep inside your abdomen, packed around your organs, behaves very differently from the softer fat just under your skin. When those deep fat cells become overfilled, they get stressed and start attracting immune cells, which switch on an inflammation program and release signalling molecules into your bloodstream. Those molecules travel everywhere, so a local problem in your belly becomes a body-wide, low-grade inflammation you can measure with a simple blood marker called CRP. This is why two people at the same weight can have very different health pictures — it's not how much fat you carry, but where, and how stressed those fat cells are.

causes.name: High-sugar / ultra-processed diet (glucose spikes & AGEs)

chain.node: High-glycemic, ultra-processed diet
chain.lay: You eat a lot of fast, refined, packaged food
chain.say: This is the starting point, so let's set the scene. A **high-glycemic** food is one whose sugars are stripped down and easy to digest, so they flood into your blood very quickly — think white bread, pastries, soft drinks, and instant noodles rather than beans or whole vegetables. **Ultra-processed** means the food has been industrially broken apart and rebuilt from refined ingredients, additives, and often deep-fried or heavily heated — the packaged, ready-to-eat stuff. When most of your week runs on food like this, you are feeding your body two things at once: a rush of fast sugar, and a load of chemicals created by that heavy processing. Everything that follows flows from those two ingredients.

chain.node: Repeated postprandial glucose spikes plus dietary intake of advanced glycation end-products (AGEs)
chain.lay: Every meal spikes your blood sugar, and the food carries sticky 'browning' chemicals
chain.say: Because that food is so refined, its sugar isn't released slowly — it pours into your bloodstream in a big surge. **Glucose** is simply the type of sugar your blood carries for energy, and a **postprandial spike** just means a sharp rise in that blood sugar in the hour or two right *after* eating (postprandial literally means 'after a meal'). On top of that surge, the high-heat cooking used to make this food — frying, grilling, roasting — creates **AGEs**, short for **advanced glycation end-products**: sticky compounds that form when sugar chemically bonds onto proteins or fats, the same reaction that browns a piece of toast. So each processed meal delivers a double hit — a flood of glucose from the inside, plus a serving of these ready-made sticky compounds from the food itself. That afternoon crash you feel is the visible edge of this surge-and-drop.

chain.node: RAGE engagement drives oxidative stress
chain.lay: These sugary, browned particles latch onto a docking point on your cells and start tiny fires
chain.say: Now those AGEs need somewhere to act — and here both halves of that double hit come together. The flood of glucose from your meal doesn't just get burned for energy; when blood sugar stays high, some of it chemically sticks onto your body's own proteins, quietly forming even more AGEs inside you, so the sticky compounds from your food are now joined by sticky compounds made within. All of them go looking for the same landing spot on your cells: a **receptor** is just a specific slot on a cell's surface, shaped so that one particular molecule fits into it like a key into a lock, and when the key lands it sends a message inside the cell. The receptor these AGEs fit is called **RAGE** (its full name is 'receptor for advanced glycation end-products'). Because your bloodstream is now carrying so many AGEs from both sources, they keep plugging into RAGE, and each time they do the cell answers by producing a burst of **oxidative stress** — an overload of unstable molecules called free radicals that snatch pieces off your cell's own parts, roughly the way rust corrodes metal or a cut apple browns in the air. In short, the sticky particles from your plate and from your own bloodstream dock onto your cells and set off a shower of this corrosive damage.

chain.node: NF-κB → TNF-α, IL-6, IL-1β
chain.lay: That damage flips on a master 'alarm switch' that pumps out inflammation signals
chain.say: That burst of oxidative damage doesn't stay quiet — inside the cell it acts like smoke reaching a smoke detector. The detector here is **NF-kappa-B**, a master control switch (a **transcription factor**, meaning a protein that decides which of the cell's genes get switched on). Because the oxidative stress from the previous step trips this switch, NF-kappa-B turns on and orders the cell to manufacture **cytokines** — the chemical messenger molecules your immune system uses to call in and coordinate inflammation. The specific ones it releases here are **TNF-alpha, IL-6, and IL-1-beta**, three of the body's main 'start inflammation now' signals. So a chemical reaction that began with your food has now reached deep inside the cell and hit the button that broadcasts an inflammation alarm.

chain.node: Chronic inflammatory tone
chain.lay: Your body ends up quietly simmering in low-grade inflammation all the time
chain.say: Here's why a single meal becomes a lasting problem. **Inflammation** is your immune system's normal emergency response — useful in short bursts to fight infection or heal an injury, and meant to switch off once the job is done. But because you eat this way meal after meal, day after day, that alarm switch keeps getting tripped and those cytokines keep getting released, so the system never gets the quiet it needs to stand down. The result is a **chronic inflammatory tone** — a constant, low-level simmer of inflammation humming in the background even when nothing is actually wrong. That is exactly why your sense of being inflamed, puffy, or run-down tends to rise and fall with how you ate that week, and it's the steady background heat that this whole chain has been building toward.

tell.symptoms: Your week runs on soft drinks, refined carbs (white bread, pastries, instant noodles), and fried or packaged food, and you get post-meal energy crashes or an afternoon slump. You may also notice that how inflamed, puffy, or achy you feel tracks closely with how you ate that particular week.
tell.labMarker: HbA1c creeping into high-normal (>5.6%); fasting glucose >5.5 mmol/L; post-meal glucose spikes on CGM >8-9 mmol/L

fixes.what: Cut added sugar and refined carbohydrate; flatten post-meal spikes with protein/fibre-first meal ordering (raise fiber_g, lower sugar_g)
Reduce dietary AGEs — favour steaming/boiling over high-heat frying, grilling and charring
Sulforaphane — activates Nrf2, the antioxidant transcription factor that antagonises NF-κB and RAGE-driven signalling
Astaxanthin — quenches the oxidative stress that AGE/RAGE engagement generates

causes.confusedWith: Undiagnosed type 2 diabetes — persistent hyperglycaemia amplifies this loop (most robust human AGE/RAGE data is in diabetics) and needs its own management
causes.keyInsight: Fast, refined, deep-fried food doesn't just spike your blood sugar for an hour — it hands your cells sticky, sugar-coated debris that trips an internal alarm switch, and that alarm quietly keeps ringing long after the meal is gone.
causes.hook: Afternoon crashes and inflammation that mirror how you ate
causes.evidence: This is a tier 2 link: the association between ultra-processed, high-sugar diets and higher inflammatory markers is strong and consistent across large studies. The exact size of each step's contribution in any one person is still being mapped, so treat it as a well-supported pattern rather than a precise dial.
causes.plain: When food is highly refined and processed, its sugars hit your bloodstream fast and in big waves, and high-heat cooking (frying, grilling, roasting) coats it in sticky compounds called AGEs. Some of that surging blood sugar also sticks onto your own proteins, quietly forming even more AGEs inside you. Those compounds latch onto a specific docking point on your cells called RAGE, which sets off a burst of internal "rusting" damage known as oxidative stress. That damage flips on a master control switch inside the cell, NF-kappa-B, which pumps out inflammation-signalling molecules like TNF-alpha, IL-6, and IL-1-beta. Do this meal after meal, week after week, and your body never fully switches the alarm off — leaving you in a constant low-grade inflammatory state.

causes.name: Physical inactivity / sedentary behaviour (myokine loss)

chain.node: Chronic physical inactivity / prolonged sitting, little muscle contraction
chain.lay: Your muscles barely contract all day
chain.say: This is where it starts. A **muscle contraction** is simply your muscle tightening and shortening to create force — it's what happens every time you stand, walk, lift, or climb stairs. When your days are mostly **sedentary** (meaning spent sitting or lying with very little movement) — a long stretch at a desk, hours on the couch, a low daily step count — your big muscles spend almost the entire day switched off, hardly contracting at all. On its own that might sound harmless, like your muscles are just resting. But as you'll see in the next step, those hard contractions were quietly doing a second job that has nothing to do with movement, and switching them off switches that job off too.

chain.node: Loss of contraction-induced myokine signalling (the transient muscle IL-6 pulse that normally drives IL-10 and IL-1ra)
chain.lay: The calming signals your muscles release during exercise go silent
chain.say: Because your muscles are barely contracting, they stop doing that hidden second job — releasing chemical messengers called **myokines**. A **myokine** is a signalling molecule (a tiny chemical note carried in your blood) that working muscle squirts out specifically when it contracts hard. The key one here is a brief burst of **IL-6** (short for interleukin-6, one of these messengers); in the exercise setting this short pulse acts as a helpful signal that tells your body to make its own anti-inflammatory brakes — molecules called **IL-10** and **IL-1ra** (two more of these messengers) whose entire job is to calm inflammation down. So during real exertion your muscles are actively releasing a 'settle down' signal. Now that you're sitting still and rarely contracting, that pulse almost never fires — and with it gone, the anti-inflammatory brakes it used to trigger simply aren't being made.

chain.node: Visceral fat accumulation and unopposed TNF-α tone
chain.lay: Belly fat builds up around your organs and starts sending alarm signals
chain.say: Because those calming signals are now missing and you're barely moving to burn off the energy you eat, the leftover fuel has to go somewhere — and your body packs it away as **visceral fat**, the fat stored deep inside your belly, wrapped around organs like your liver and gut (this is different from the softer fat that sits just under your skin). This kind of fat isn't inert padding that only stores energy; it behaves almost like a busy organ, constantly leaking an inflammatory signal called **TNF-alpha** (short for tumour necrosis factor-alpha, a messenger that tells nearby cells to switch into an inflamed, alarmed state). Normally the anti-inflammatory brakes from the previous step would push back against this signal and keep it in check. But because your idle muscles stopped producing those brakes, the TNF-alpha alarm is now **unopposed** — meaning there's nothing left to counter it — so its inflammatory push builds and runs unchecked.

chain.node: NF-κB-driven cytokine output no longer restrained
chain.lay: A master inflammation switch inside your cells gets stuck in the 'on' position
chain.say: Because that TNF-alpha alarm is now going unopposed, it keeps flipping a control switch deep inside your cells called **NF-kB**. Think of **NF-kB** as a master on-off switch that, when activated, tells the cell to start producing **cytokines** — the general family name for these inflammatory messenger molecules that cells use to call in and coordinate an immune response. Normally NF-kB flicks on briefly to fight a real threat like an infection and then flicks back off. But with TNF-alpha constantly prodding it and no braking signal to shut it down, the switch is left stuck on. That means your cells keep churning out inflammatory cytokines around the clock, even though there's no infection or injury to fight — the alarm is ringing with nobody to turn it off.

chain.node: Low-grade systemic inflammation, raised CRP
chain.lay: Your whole body ends up simmering with constant low-level inflammation
chain.say: Because that master switch is stuck on and inflammatory cytokines keep pouring out day after day, your body settles into a state of **low-grade systemic inflammation**. 'Low-grade' means it's mild — not the hot, red, swollen inflammation of a sprained ankle — and 'systemic' means it's spread throughout your whole body rather than at one spot. It's a quiet, constant simmer rather than a blaze. One way doctors can actually see it is by measuring **CRP** (C-reactive protein, a substance your liver makes in response to inflammatory signals); when this smoulder is present, your **CRP** level in a simple blood test creeps upward. That raised CRP is the measurable fingerprint of the whole chain — muscles that stopped contracting, calming signals that fell silent, fat that built up and alarmed, and a switch left running — now showing up as the chronic inflammation you set out to fix.

tell.symptoms: Is this you? Most of your day is spent sitting — a desk, a screen, a couch — with a low daily step count, often more than eight hours seated and little or no resistance training. You may notice you feel out of shape and generally achy, with soreness that eases while you're training consistently and quietly creeps back whenever you take a long break.
tell.labMarker: hs-CRP elevated with low cardiorespiratory fitness (low VO2/step count); improves across a training block

fixes.what: Regular endurance (zone-2) training — the modality with the strongest CRP-lowering signal, partly via building muscle and stripping visceral fat
Resistance training to raise muscle mass and restore contraction-induced myokine (IL-6 → IL-10/IL-1ra) signalling
Break up sitting with movement snacks and a daily step target — sedentary time is inflammatory independent of a formal workout

causes.confusedWith: Overlaps heavily with visceral adiposity (they reinforce each other); note the weight-independent CRP benefit of exercise is real but modest and mixed in trials (the INFLAME RCT was null without weight loss)
causes.keyInsight: Your muscles aren't just for moving — every time they contract hard, they release chemical "calm-down" signals into your blood. Sit still for years and those signals go quiet, so nothing is left to switch inflammation off.
causes.hook: Sitting all day, low steps, soreness that creeps back
causes.evidence: This sits at a strong-association tier: large studies reliably link sedentary time and low fitness with higher inflammatory markers, and we understand the muscle-signalling mechanism well, though inactivity usually travels together with other factors (weight gain, poor sleep, diet) that make the exact size of its independent effect harder to pin down.
causes.plain: Working muscle is secretly a signalling organ. When muscle fibres contract hard, they release short-lived messenger chemicals called myokines — including a brief pulse of IL-6 that, in this exercise context, tells your body to produce anti-inflammatory brakes like IL-10 and IL-1ra. When you barely move, that pulse rarely fires, so the brakes are never applied. At the same time, unburned energy is stored as visceral fat around your organs, which leaks its own inflammatory signal (TNF-alpha) with nothing to oppose it. The result is a master inflammation switch (NF-kB) left running and a steady, low-grade simmer of inflammation that shows up as a raised CRP blood level.

causes.name: Gut dysbiosis / metabolic endotoxemia (leaky gut)

chain.node: Low-fibre diet & dysbiosis thinning the gut barrier
chain.lay: A low-fibre diet weakens your gut wall until it starts to leak
chain.say: The inside of your gut is lined by a wall just **one cell thick** — think of it as the fence between the food-and-bacteria world inside your intestines and the rest of your body. **Fibre** is the part of plants your own body can't digest, but the **friendly bacteria** living in your gut feed on it and, in return, produce fuel that keeps that one-cell-thick wall well-nourished and tightly sealed. When you eat little fibre and few vegetables, you starve those friendly bacteria, and their population shifts into an unhealthy mix — a state doctors call **dysbiosis** (simply an out-of-balance gut community). Without their fuel, the gaps between the wall's cells loosen and the barrier thins, a condition popularly known as **leaky gut**. So the very first domino is dietary: less fibre means weaker gut bacteria, which means a fence that no longer holds the line.

chain.node: Bacterial LPS translocates into blood (metabolic endotoxemia), partly on dietary-fat chylomicrons
chain.lay: Bits of bacteria slip through the leaky wall into your blood
chain.say: Because that fence is now leaky, things that were always meant to stay locked inside your intestines can push through into your bloodstream. The main troublemaker is **LPS** (short for lipopolysaccharide), a tiny **molecule** — a molecule is simply one of the minuscule building-block particles that everything, including bacteria, is made of — that coats the outer surface of many gut bacteria; to your body it's a red-flag signature that screams *bacteria are where they shouldn't be*. When LPS crosses the weakened wall and enters your blood, that low-level leak is called **metabolic endotoxemia** — literally, a small steady amount of bacterial toxin (endotoxin) circulating in your blood. Fat makes this worse: when you eat a **high-fat meal**, your gut packages the fat into tiny transport bubbles called **chylomicrons**, and LPS can hitch a ride on these bubbles straight into circulation. That's exactly why your symptoms tend to flare after fatty, processed meals — more fat means more of these bubbles ferrying bacterial fragments across the leaky border.

chain.node: TLR4 activation on immune cells
chain.lay: Your immune cells spot the bacterial bits and hit the alarm
chain.say: Now that LPS is loose in your blood, your body's defence system notices immediately. Your **immune cells** — the patrol cells whose job is to hunt for anything foreign or dangerous — carry a specific sensor on their surface called **TLR4** (a **receptor**, meaning a docking-point molecule shaped to recognise one particular thing). TLR4 is tuned to lock onto LPS specifically, so the moment bacterial LPS drifts past, TLR4 grabs it and flips the immune cell into full alert. This step only happens *because* the previous one delivered LPS into the blood — no leak, no LPS, nothing for TLR4 to grab. In effect, your immune system reads the circulating bacterial fragments as proof of an infection and sounds the alarm, even though there's no real invading army to fight.

chain.node: NF-κB → pro-inflammatory cytokines
chain.lay: The alarm switches on genes that pour out inflammation signals
chain.say: Once TLR4 has locked onto LPS and the immune cell is on alert, that alarm has to be relayed to the cell's control centre to actually do something. It does this by switching on a master control switch inside the cell called **NF-κB** — think of it as a foreman who walks into the cell's command room and turns on a whole set of emergency instructions. Those instructions tell the cell to manufacture **cytokines**, which are small **proteins** — proteins are the tiny worker-molecules your cells build to get jobs done — that immune cells use like signal-flares to shout messages to one another. In this case they're **pro-inflammatory cytokines**, meaning their message is *start inflammation now*. **Inflammation** itself is just your body's normal emergency response — the heat, swelling, and burst of immune activity meant to fight threats and repair damage. So the LPS alarm from the previous step is what throws the NF-κB switch, and the switch is what floods your system with these inflammation-triggering messengers.

chain.node: Persistent systemic inflammation
chain.lay: The alarm never switches off, so your whole body stays inflamed
chain.say: Here's the trap: inflammation is meant to be a short, sharp response that flips off once the threat is gone. But your leaky gut is leaking LPS **all the time**, so TLR4 keeps getting triggered, NF-κB keeps getting switched on, and the pro-inflammatory cytokines keep getting released day after day. The result is **persistent systemic inflammation** — *persistent* meaning it never resolves, and *systemic* meaning it's spread through your whole body rather than parked in one spot. This is the low, simmering, background inflammation that's linked to feeling puffy, achy, and run-down, and over years to many chronic conditions. The crucial takeaway is that nothing here is fighting a real infection — your immune system is stuck responding to a leak, and the only lasting way to quiet it is to reseal the gut wall by feeding the friendly bacteria that keep it tight.

tell.symptoms: You often feel bloated, your stools are irregular, and you notice more food sensitivities than you used to. You may feel achy, puffy, or run-down for a day or two after a heavy, high-fat or highly processed meal — and you tend to eat little fibre and few vegetables.
tell.labMarker: Elevated LPS-binding protein (LBP) or soluble CD14 if available; low stool microbial diversity

fixes.what: Raise fermentable fibre and prebiotics (raise fiber_g) to feed barrier-protective, butyrate-producing bacteria
Add fermented foods and polyphenol-rich plants to diversify the microbiome
Reduce chronic high-fat/high-sugar loads that drive dysbiosis and barrier leak
Omega-3 — supports tight-junction integrity and lowers endotoxin-driven cytokine output

causes.confusedWith: IBD or coeliac disease — if there is blood, weight loss or severe pain, this is not simple low-grade dysbiosis and needs work-up
causes.keyInsight: When your gut wall gets leaky, bits of your own gut bacteria slip into your bloodstream — and your immune system treats that leak like an ongoing invasion, keeping the whole body quietly inflamed.
causes.hook: Bloating, food sensitivities, and flares after fatty meals
causes.evidence: This is a strong, well-documented association: people with a leaky gut and signs of bacterial toxins in the blood reliably show higher inflammation, and the individual biological steps are all established. What is still being pinned down is exactly how much of any one person's chronic inflammation this pathway is driving versus other causes.
causes.plain: Your gut lining is a single-cell-thick wall that lets nutrients through but is supposed to keep bacteria out. A diet low in fibre starves the friendly bacteria that normally keep this wall sealed and well-fed, so the wall thins and becomes leaky. Fragments of bacteria — especially a molecule called LPS from their outer coat — then slip into your bloodstream, sometimes hitching a ride on the fat droplets you absorb from a fatty meal. Your immune cells detect LPS as a danger signal and switch on an inflammation program, and because the leak is constant, that program never fully shuts off — leaving you in a state of low, persistent, whole-body inflammation.

causes.name: Chronic stress & short sleep (glucocorticoid resistance)

chain.node: Chronic psychological stress / habitual sleep <6h
chain.lay: Life stays stressful, and sleep stays short
chain.say: This whole chain starts with something completely ordinary: weeks where your mind won't switch off and you're regularly getting **less than six hours of sleep**. By **chronic stress** we simply mean stress that doesn't let up — not one bad afternoon, but a steady state of pressure that runs for days or weeks. Your body was designed to handle short bursts of stress and then return to calm, so it treats occasional stress as harmless. The problem begins only when there's no return to calm — when the pressure and the short nights become your normal setting rather than a rare exception.

chain.node: Prolonged cortisol exposure
chain.lay: Your main stress hormone stays switched on far too long
chain.say: Because that stress and sleep loss never let up, your body keeps releasing **cortisol**, which is your main **stress hormone** — a hormone being a chemical messenger your body makes in one place and sends through your blood to tell other parts what to do. Normally cortisol follows a daily rhythm: it rises in the morning to get you going and falls at night so you can rest, with plenty of quiet, low-cortisol hours in between. But because the stress and short nights from the step before never switch off, that natural evening dip never fully happens, so cortisol stays **elevated around the clock**. The key change here isn't a single big spike — it's the loss of the quiet periods, leaving your cells bathed in cortisol far longer than nature intended.

chain.node: Glucocorticoid-receptor resistance in immune cells
chain.lay: Your immune cells stop listening to the calming signal
chain.say: Here's the twist that constant cortisol creates. Cortisol only works by docking onto a **receptor** — think of a receptor as a lock on the surface of a cell that a specific messenger acts as the key for, and when the key turns the lock, the cell follows the instruction. The cells that matter here are your **immune cells**, the defence cells that run inflammation. When those cells are flooded with cortisol nonstop, they protect themselves by pulling back and dulling those locks — a state called **glucocorticoid-receptor resistance** ("glucocorticoid" is just the technical family name for cortisol). In plain terms: because the calming signal never stopped, your immune cells stopped listening to it, the same way you'd tune out an alarm that never turns off.

chain.node: Cortisol can no longer brake NF-κB → IL-6, CRP rise
chain.lay: The brake on inflammation goes missing
chain.say: Now that your immune cells have stopped listening to cortisol, cortisol loses its most important job — putting out inflammation. Inside those cells sits a master switch called **NF-κB**, a **protein** (a tiny molecular machine built by your cells) that acts as the on-button for inflammation: flip it on and the cell starts producing inflammatory signals. Cortisol's normal role is to hold that switch down, acting as a **brake**. But with those receptors now dulled, cortisol can no longer press the brake, so NF-κB stays switched on and pumps out messengers like **IL-6** — a signal that spreads inflammation — which in turn drives your **liver**, the large organ that manages much of your blood chemistry, to release **CRP**, or C-reactive protein, a substance doctors measure precisely because it rises when inflammation is active.

chain.node: Elevated inflammatory markers
chain.lay: Your inflammation readings climb
chain.say: Because that brake is gone and NF-κB keeps churning out IL-6 and CRP, the end result is measurable: **elevated inflammatory markers** in your blood. "Inflammatory markers" are simply the substances, like **CRP**, that a blood test uses to gauge how much background inflammation you're carrying — a higher number means the fire is burning hotter. This is the quiet, low-grade kind of inflammation that doesn't announce itself with obvious swelling, which is why it can climb without you feeling any single dramatic symptom. It does show up, though, as the worse recovery, extra soreness, and stress-linked CRP bumps from the symptom list — the visible fingerprint of a brake that stress and short sleep quietly disabled.

tell.symptoms: You feel persistently stressed and sleep less than six hours, often lying "wired but tired" — exhausted yet unable to switch off. During your most stressful or under-slept stretches, workouts leave you more sore than usual, recovery drags, and if you've had bloodwork you may notice your inflammation marker (CRP) creeps up in exactly the weeks life felt hardest.
tell.labMarker: IL-6 and hs-CRP rise after ≥3 nights of curtailed sleep; consider a flattened diurnal cortisol curve on salivary testing

fixes.what: Protect 7-8h sleep — even a few nights of restriction measurably raises IL-6 and CRP
Daily stress down-regulation (breathwork, zone-2 cardio, time outdoors) to restore glucocorticoid sensitivity
Consistent sleep/wake timing to stabilise the cortisol rhythm that gates inflammation
Boswellia — inhibits 5-LOX and dampens NF-κB signalling for symptomatic relief while root drivers are addressed

causes.confusedWith: Clinical depression or an anxiety disorder, which share the raised-cytokine picture and may need direct treatment
causes.keyInsight: Cortisol is your body's built-in "off switch" for inflammation — but when stress and short sleep keep it switched on around the clock, your cells stop listening to it, and the fire it was supposed to put out quietly keeps burning.
causes.hook: Wired but tired, sore for days, stress that won't switch off?
causes.evidence: This is a tier 2 pathway: the link between chronic stress, short sleep, and higher inflammatory markers is backed by strong and repeated human studies, and the "receptor resistance" mechanism is well described in immune cells. It sits just below a fully settled mechanism because how strongly it plays out varies a lot from person to person.
causes.plain: Cortisol is the main stress hormone, and one of its most important everyday jobs is to calm inflammation — it's your natural brake pedal. Normal life gives cortisol a rhythm: high in the morning, low at night, with quiet periods where the brake can reset. Chronic stress and habitually short sleep flatten that rhythm, keeping cortisol elevated for far too long. Just as a smoke alarm you can never silence eventually gets ignored, your immune cells stop responding to constant cortisol — the brake still exists, but nobody's pushing it anymore. With the brake gone, low-grade inflammation is free to simmer, which is why your inflammatory markers climb during your hardest, most sleep-starved weeks.

causes.name: Inflammaging (senescent-cell burden)

chain.node: Ageing, cumulative DNA/oxidative damage
chain.lay: Years of wear and tear quietly damage your cells
chain.say: Every cell in your body carries **DNA** — the full instruction manual, written in molecules, that tells the cell how to build and repair itself. Just by living, that manual takes daily hits: some come from ordinary copying mistakes every time a cell divides to make a fresh cell, and a lot come from **oxidative damage**, which simply means the wear caused by reactive oxygen fragments — unstable leftovers your cells produce whenever they burn fuel for energy, a bit like microscopic rust building up from running an engine. A single hit is easily patched by the cell's built-in repair machinery, but the key word here is **cumulative** — the small amounts of damage that never get repaired slowly add up over many years. So the starting point isn't one dramatic injury; it's decades of tiny, unfixed scratches to your cells' instruction manuals piling up.

chain.node: Accumulation of senescent cells
chain.lay: Damaged cells stop dividing but refuse to die
chain.say: Because that DNA damage has quietly piled up, your body faces a problem: a badly damaged cell that keeps copying itself could turn into a tumour, so your body has a safety brake. When a cell crosses a damage threshold, it flips into a locked state called **senescence**, meaning it permanently stops dividing — these are **senescent cells**, sometimes nicknamed 'zombie cells' because they're neither properly alive-and-working nor cleared away and gone. Normally your **immune system** — your body's clean-up and defence crew — mops these up. But as you age it clears them less efficiently, so instead of being removed they slowly **accumulate** in your tissues (the general word for the body's living material, like muscle, fat, and the linings of organs). The result is a growing population of these stuck, un-cleared cells sitting quietly inside you.

chain.node: Senescence-associated secretory phenotype (SASP)
chain.lay: These stuck cells start broadcasting chemical alarm signals
chain.say: Here's the twist that makes those accumulating cells matter: a senescent cell doesn't just sit there silently. Because it's damaged and stuck, it switches on a distinctive behaviour that scientists call the **senescence-associated secretory phenotype**, or **SASP** — which sounds intimidating but literally just means 'the pattern of chemicals that senescent cells secrete'. **Secrete** simply means to release out into the surrounding tissue, the way a gland releases sweat. So each of these stuck cells starts steadily pumping out a cocktail of signalling molecules — chemical messages — into the space around it. One zombie cell's output is tiny, but remember they've been accumulating, so you now have many of them all broadcasting at once.

chain.node: Steady IL-6, IL-1β, TNF-α secretion
chain.lay: Those alarm signals are the body's inflammation chemicals
chain.say: Now, what exactly are those signals? A large part of the SASP cocktail is made of **cytokines** — small proteins (a protein is one of the basic worker-molecules cells build) that act as the immune system's messaging service, telling nearby cells to switch inflammation on or off. Three of the loudest ones released here are named **IL-6**, **IL-1β** and **TNF-α** — you don't need the chemistry behind the names, only that all three are pro-inflammatory, meaning their job is to shout 'start inflammation here'. Because the senescent cells never stop and never die, they release these three in a slow, **steady** trickle rather than a short useful burst. So the alarm doesn't get to reset the way it does after, say, a normal workout or a healed cut — it just keeps quietly sounding.

chain.node: Chronic 'inflammaging'
chain.lay: A low background hum of inflammation you can never fully switch off
chain.say: Put the chain together and you get the final effect. **Inflammation** is normally a short-term, targeted response — your body floods an injured or infected spot with immune activity to fix it, then shuts it off once the job is done. But because your senescent cells are steadily leaking IL-6, IL-1β and TNF-α with no off-switch, that fixing signal never fully powers down anywhere. The outcome is a permanent, body-wide, low-level inflammation that researchers named **inflammaging** — a blend of 'inflammation' and 'ageing'. This is the 'floor' you can feel: because your body is already carrying a small inflammation load before you add anything, the same training and life stress now leaves you slightly achier and slower to recover, even when your weight, diet, movement and sleep are all perfectly dialled in.

tell.symptoms: Is this you? Your baseline achiness has crept up and you recover slower than you used to, even though your weight, diet, movement and sleep are all dialled in — like there's a low 'floor' of inflammation that never fully clears no matter what you do.
tell.labMarker: Gradually creeping hs-CRP and IL-6 with age with no acute cause; no single confirmatory clinical test

fixes.what: Regular exercise — reduces senescent-cell markers and SASP output in ageing tissue
Sulforaphane — Nrf2 activation counters the oxidative stress that drives senescence
Astaxanthin — antioxidant that blunts SASP-associated oxidative signalling
Omega-3 — associated (observationally) with longer telomeres and lower inflammatory-ageing markers

causes.confusedWith: An occult chronic infection or early autoimmune disease, which can mimic 'unexplained' age-related CRP elevation and should be excluded first. Note: causal proof that clearing senescent cells lowers human systemic inflammation is still largely animal/early-trial, so this sits at tier 2, not mechanistic-confirmed.
causes.keyInsight: As you age, a small pool of worn-out cells stops dividing but refuses to die — and instead of sitting quietly, they leak a steady drip of inflammation chemicals that raises your baseline no matter how clean your diet, sleep and training are. This is why recovery slowly gets harder with age even when you do everything right: you're not fighting one big fire, you're living next to a low, constant background hum.
causes.hook: Recovery slows with age even when everything else is dialled in.
causes.evidence: Evidence tier 2 (strong association). The build-up of these worn-out cells and their inflammation chemicals is very consistently linked to age-related inflammation across large studies, and clearing them lowers inflammation in animals — but proof that reducing them fixes the problem in healthy humans is still being established.
causes.plain: Over a lifetime, some of your cells accumulate enough internal damage that they permanently stop dividing but don't get cleared away — these are called senescent cells. Rather than staying silent, they switch into a mode where they constantly release inflammation-signalling chemicals into the surrounding tissue. Individually each cell releases very little, but because they slowly accumulate and never stop, together they create a permanent low-grade background of inflammation — a phenomenon researchers named 'inflammaging' (inflammation plus ageing). This background is why the same training or diet that once left you fresh now leaves you slightly achier and slower to bounce back, because your body is already running with a small inflammation load before you add any new stress on top.

ladder: The surface complaint: bloating, gas, irregular stools, cramping, sluggish digestion
The proximate state: a gut lining that's inflamed or under-fuelled, gas being overproduced, or motility/secretion running too fast or too slow
The upstream drivers: a microbiome starved of fibre diversity, a stress axis stuck 'on', bacteria growing where they shouldn't, a food you can't fully digest, or bile acids spilling into the colon
The root behaviours: how varied your plants are, how you handle stress and sleep, how you space meals, which gut-disrupting meds you take, and what your ileum and bile flow are doing
gut-health.intro: "Poor gut health" is almost never one thing — it's a final common symptom (bloat, gas, unpredictable stools) that several very different engines can drive, and the whole point of diagnosing yourself is to find out which engine is yours. In one person the microbiome has run short of fibre diversity, so the bacteria that make butyrate — the fuel that keeps the colon lining fed and sealed — have thinned out. In another, a chronically switched-on stress axis is yanking the gut's motility and pain sensitivity around through the brain-gut nerves, so a normal amount of gas feels like agony. In a third, bacteria that belong in the colon have crept up into the small intestine and ferment food the moment it arrives (SIBO), producing gas within an hour of eating. In a fourth, it's a specific carbohydrate — lactose or the FODMAP family — that never gets absorbed and instead feeds a fermentation bloom. In a subset with stubborn diarrhoea, bile acids the ileum should have reclaimed are spilling into the colon — a common, treatable cause that gets mislabelled as IBS for years. And in many people a quiet culprit is a medication (acid blockers, repeated antibiotics, NSAIDs) or an ultra-processed, low-fibre diet steadily eroding the terrain. The fixes for these barely overlap — more fibre helps the first and can worsen the third — so read the 'how to tell' for each and hunt for the pattern that sounds like your gut, not the average gut. One rule before self-treating: persistent symptoms with any red flag (blood in stool, unintended weight loss, iron deficiency, onset after 50, family history of coeliac/IBD/bowel cancer) need a doctor and testing first, not a supplement.

causes.name: Dysbiosis / low fibre diversity

chain.node: Low plant and fibre variety in the diet
chain.lay: You eat the same few beige foods, day after day
chain.say: This is where it starts, so there's nothing before it to blame — just the everyday pattern of a narrow diet. **Fibre** is the part of plants — the stringy, structural material in vegetables, fruit, beans and whole grains — that your own digestive system genuinely cannot break down or absorb, so it passes all the way through to your large intestine intact. Here's the key point most people miss: there isn't one single fibre, there are many different kinds, and each type of plant carries its own mix. That means **variety** matters as much as amount — eating a big bowl of white rice and bread every day gives you very little fibre and almost no diversity of it. When your meals stay **monotonous and low in plants**, only a small handful of fibre types ever reach the deeper parts of your gut.

chain.node: Too few fermentable substrates → butyrate-producing species thin out
chain.lay: Your gut's helpful bacteria run out of food and thin out
chain.say: Because so few kinds of fibre are arriving from your narrow diet, the bacteria waiting downstream simply don't get fed. Living in your large intestine are trillions of bacteria — collectively called your **gut microbiome**, meaning the whole community of microbes that live in your digestive tract — and many of the helpful ones survive by eating fibre. The way they eat it is called **fermentation**, which just means breaking food down without oxygen, the same basic process that turns grapes into wine; the fibre they feast on is their **fermentable substrate**, a plain term for 'the raw material they can actually digest.' Crucially, different bacteria prefer different fibres, so when only a couple of fibre types show up, the specialists that need the missing ones have nothing to eat. In particular, the **butyrate-producing species** — the bacteria whose job is to make a specific fuel called butyrate — get starved out and dwindle in number.

chain.node: Butyrate / short-chain fatty acids fall
chain.lay: A key fuel your gut makes, called butyrate, drops
chain.say: Because those specialist bacteria have thinned out, the substance they used to manufacture starts to run short. That substance is **butyrate**, one of a small family of molecules called **short-chain fatty acids** — think of them as tiny energy-rich packets that the bacteria release as a by-product when they ferment your fibre. In a well-fed gut, this is a beautiful trade: you hand your bacteria fibre you couldn't use, and they hand you back butyrate that your body very much can use. But butyrate isn't something you can eat directly in any meaningful amount — you have to grow the bacteria that make it, by feeding them fibre. So when the fibre variety collapses and the butyrate-makers shrink, your **butyrate and short-chain fatty acid levels fall**, and the steady supply your gut has come to rely on quietly dries up.

chain.node: Colonocytes lose their main fuel (butyrate supplies ~70% of their energy); mucus layer thins, barrier and tight junctions weaken
chain.lay: The cells lining your gut get starved and the wall turns leaky
chain.say: Because butyrate has fallen, the cells that depend on it are suddenly left short of fuel. The lining of your large intestine is built from cells called **colonocytes** — simply 'colon cells,' the single layer that forms the inner surface of your gut wall. Here's what makes them unusual: while most cells in your body run on sugar (**glucose**, the simple sugar carried in your blood), colonocytes run mainly on butyrate delivered right next door by your bacteria — it supplies roughly **70% of their energy**. So when butyrate drops, these cells are effectively going hungry in a way most of your body never is. Underfed colonocytes can't keep up their maintenance work, and two protections weaken as a result. First, the **mucus layer** — a slippery protective gel that normally coats the gut wall and keeps bacteria at arm's length — grows thin; and second, the **tight junctions**, the microscopic 'stitching' that seals neighbouring cells together into a solid, leak-proof **barrier**, start to loosen, so the wall that should stay firmly shut becomes a little leaky.

chain.node: Bloating, irregularity, low-grade gut inflammation
chain.lay: You feel bloated, irregular, and mildly inflamed
chain.say: Because the mucus coat has thinned and the wall's seams have loosened, the calm, well-defended gut you're meant to have becomes irritable and less predictable. With the bacterial community disturbed and butyrate low, the muscle movements that gently march food along can lose their steady rhythm, which shows up as **irregularity** — hard, infrequent or unpredictable stools. At the same time, a thinner mucus layer and looser seams let bacteria and their fragments brush up against the gut wall in a way they normally wouldn't, and your **immune system** — your body's built-in defence force — reacts with a persistent, low-level **inflammation**, meaning mild ongoing irritation and swelling in the tissue rather than a dramatic illness. The trapped gas from all this sluggish, uneven fermentation is felt as **bloating** — that tight, over-full feeling in your belly. And this is exactly why adding a wider range of plants tends to help — though the extra fibre can briefly increase gas at first, as your hungry bacteria wake up and get back to work.

tell.symptoms: Is this you? Your bloating, hard or infrequent stools and general gut grumbling seem to track with a monotonous, beige, low-plant diet, and they ease when you add more vegetables, legumes and whole grains — even if gas briefly gets worse first. You tend to eat a fairly narrow range of foods and rarely reach for a wide variety of plants.
tell.labMarker: Stool microbiome test showing low alpha (Shannon) diversity and depleted butyrate producers (Faecalibacterium, Roseburia); low faecal SCFA. No validated cut-off and the link to symptoms is largely observational — read as a pattern, not a diagnosis.

fixes.what: Aim for 30+ different plant species per week (rotate vegetables, legumes, nuts, seeds, whole grains) — diversity of fibre matters more than total grams; note this target comes from observational data (American Gut Project), not a trial
Add a progressive prebiotic fibre — start low and ramp to avoid gas
Daily fermented foods (yoghurt, kefir, kimchi) — the best-evidenced way to raise diversity and lower inflammatory markers; oral butyrate is an optional adjunct with only limited human data
Cut added sugar and ultra-processed foods, which crowd out fibre and favour low-diversity communities
A multi-strain probiotic during the rebuild phase — evidence is modest and strain-specific

causes.confusedWith: SIBO — where adding fibre makes bloating worse, not better
causes.keyInsight: Your gut bacteria don't eat the food you eat — they eat the plant fibre you can't digest. The narrower and more beige your diet, the fewer of these helpful bacteria survive to make the fuel that keeps your gut wall fed, sealed and calm.
causes.hook: Bloated, irregular, and stuck eating the same few foods?
causes.evidence: This is a strong, well-replicated association (evidence tier 2): diets low in plant and fibre variety consistently line up with fewer butyrate-producing gut bacteria and more digestive complaints. The exact cause-and-effect isn't nailed down in every individual, but the link is robust and tends to improve when people widen their fibre intake.
causes.plain: Deep in your large intestine lives a community of trillions of bacteria — your "gut microbiome" — and many of the helpful ones survive by feeding on plant fibre that your own body can't break down. When your diet is monotonous and low in plants, only a few kinds of fibre arrive, so the bacteria that specialise in making a fuel called butyrate slowly starve and shrink in number. With fewer of them working, butyrate levels drop, and that matters because butyrate is the main energy source for the very cells that line your gut wall. Underfed, that lining thins its protective mucus coat and its seams loosen, which shows up as bloating, irregularity and a low simmer of gut inflammation. The encouraging part: feeding those bacteria a wider variety of plants usually rebuilds their numbers and their fuel supply.

causes.name: Stress / gut-brain axis dysregulation (IBS spectrum)

chain.node: Chronic psychological stress / poor sleep
chain.lay: Ongoing stress and bad sleep keep your body on high alert
chain.say: This whole chain starts not in your stomach but in your head. When life piles on — deadlines, worry, argument, week after week of short or broken sleep — your body reads it as a **chronic** (meaning long-lasting, not a one-off) **stress** state and stays braced, as if danger might arrive at any moment. Your body cannot easily tell the difference between a physical threat and a mental one, so a stressful inbox and a genuine emergency switch on the same internal alarm. Poor sleep makes this worse, because sleep is when your nervous system is supposed to power down and reset, and without it the alarm never fully switches off. Nothing has happened in your gut yet — but the signal that will reach it has just been set in motion.

chain.node: HPA axis and CRF (corticotropin-releasing factor) signalling switch on
chain.lay: Your brain flips on the body's main stress-alarm system
chain.say: Because that sense of ongoing threat is now switched on, your brain reaches for its central stress-response system, known as the **HPA axis**. The name is just a chain of three body parts that pass a message down the line: the **hypothalamus** (a control centre deep in your brain), the **pituitary** (a pea-sized **gland** — a small organ whose job is to release chemical messengers), and the **adrenal glands** (two small glands sitting on top of your kidneys). The very first messenger the hypothalamus fires off is called **CRF**, short for corticotropin-releasing factor — think of it as the starter pistol that tells the rest of the chain to go. These messengers are **hormones**, meaning chemicals that travel in your bloodstream to carry instructions from one part of the body to another. So the mental pressure from Step 1 has now been converted into a physical, chemical signal spreading through your body.

chain.node: Cortisol + mucosal mast-cell mediators (histamine, proteases) sensitise visceral afferents
chain.lay: Stress chemicals make your gut's pain nerves touchy
chain.say: Because the alarm chain from Step 2 has fired, two things now flood your gut. First, the adrenal glands release **cortisol**, the body's main stress **hormone**, which keeps you keyed up and primes your **tissues** (the body's sheets of living cells) to react. Second, that stress signal pokes special immune cells sitting in your gut wall called **mast cells** — an **immune cell** is one of your body's defence units, and mast cells are little storage packets that burst open and spill their contents when provoked. What they spill is **histamine** (the same irritating chemical behind an allergic itch or swelling) and **proteases** (which are **enzymes** — **proteins**, the body's tiny molecular machines, that here speed up chemical reactions by snipping neighbouring proteins apart). This chemical cocktail washes over your **visceral afferents**, the sensory nerves that carry sensation from your internal organs up to your brain, and **sensitises** them — meaning it lowers their trigger point so they fire off at the gentlest touch. Your gut's warning system has just had its volume dial cranked right up.

chain.node: Motility speeds up or stalls, tight junctions loosen, visceral pain nerves become hypersensitive
chain.lay: Your gut's movement, barrier and pain wiring all go haywire
chain.say: Now that those nerves and tissues are chemically wound up from Step 3, the physical workings of your gut start to misbehave in three ways at once. The **motility** — the rhythmic squeezing of muscle that pushes food along — either races ahead too fast or grinds to a stall, because the same stress chemicals that sensitise nerves also disturb this muscle timing. The **tight junctions**, which are the glue-like seals between the cells lining your gut wall that normally keep the barrier snug, loosen slightly, making the lining a touch leakier and a touch more irritable. And the **visceral pain nerves** from the last step are now so **hypersensitive** that a completely normal amount of gas or stretch — something you would usually never feel — gets read as a real problem. Nothing has been damaged; the settings on your gut have simply been knocked out of balance.

chain.node: Bloating, cramping, urgency or constipation, pain out of proportion to gas
chain.lay: You feel bloating, cramping and unpredictable bowels — with pain out of all proportion
chain.say: Because your gut is now moving erratically, sealed a little loosely, and wired to over-report pain, all of that finally surfaces as how you actually feel. When the muscle races, you get **diarrhoea and urgency** — that sudden must-go-now feeling; when it stalls, you get **constipation**, which is why many people ping-pong between the two. Because the pain nerves are turned up so high, an ordinary pocket of gas that should pass unnoticed instead lands as **cramping and bloating**, and the discomfort feels wildly out of proportion to the small meal or trapped air that caused it. This is the honest signature of the condition: the pain is completely real, yet it is driven by an over-sensitive alarm rather than by anything harmful in your gut — which is exactly why it eases when the stress from Step 1 finally lifts.

tell.symptoms: Do your flare-ups line up with deadlines, worry or bad sleep, and then quietly settle when you are on holiday? Do you swing between diarrhoea and constipation, with bloating and pain that feel far bigger than whatever you actually ate — often turning up alongside anxiety?
tell.labMarker: No gut lab confirms this — it's a clinical/pattern diagnosis (Rome IV criteria for IBS, only after red flags are excluded). A disrupted salivary cortisol rhythm is suggestive but not diagnostic.

fixes.what: Daily down-regulation: 5–10 min diaphragmatic/slow breathing or vagal work before meals to shift out of sympathetic dominance
Fix sleep regularity and total hours — poor sleep directly amplifies visceral hypersensitivity
Don't eat while stressed or rushed; sit, slow down, chew — motility is state-dependent
Support the barrier while the axis is calming — modest evidence (best data are in post-infectious IBS-D)
Gut-directed hypnotherapy or CBT is the highest-evidence intervention for stress-driven IBS (~70% respond; recommended by NICE and the AGA)

causes.confusedWith: Food intolerance — both flare after meals, but stress-driven symptoms track mood/sleep rather than a specific reproducible food
causes.keyInsight: Your gut has its own dense web of nerves wired straight to your brain, so when your mind is under pressure your bowel genuinely feels it — the bloating and pain are real, even when there is nothing wrong with the food you ate.
causes.hook: Your gut flares up exactly when life gets stressful.
causes.evidence: This sits at a strong, well-documented level of association: study after study links chronic stress and poor sleep to worse IBS-type gut symptoms, and calming the nervous system reliably eases the gut. It stops just short of being a single proven on-off switch, because the gut and brain talk in both directions and the exact pattern differs from person to person.
causes.plain: In irritable bowel syndrome (IBS), the machinery of the gut is physically normal — a scan or scope finds no ulcer, no damage — yet it behaves as if something is wrong. The reason is the gut-brain axis, a constant two-way conversation between your brain and the nervous system woven through your gut wall. When you are stressed or sleep-deprived, your brain sends alarm signals downward that speed up or stall the gut's muscle, loosen its lining, and turn up the volume on its pain nerves. So ordinary amounts of gas and stretching that you would normally never notice start registering as cramping, urgency and bloating. This is why the symptoms are genuine and yet track your stress levels rather than your diet.

causes.name: Food intolerance (FODMAPs / lactose)

chain.node: High-FODMAP foods or lactose intake
chain.lay: You eat a food loaded with certain hard-to-absorb sugars
chain.say: It starts with a specific kind of meal. Some foods are rich in **FODMAPs** — a nickname for a family of short, poorly-absorbed **carbohydrates** (the food group we call carbs, which are just sugars and starches). The everyday examples are the milk sugar **lactose** in dairy, the fruit sugar **fructose** in things like apples and honey, and the sugars packed into wheat, onion, garlic, and beans. On its own this is just eating — nothing has gone wrong yet — but you've now delivered a big load of these particular sugars into your gut, and what happens next depends on whether your body can absorb them.

chain.node: Sugar not absorbed — e.g. lactase deficiency, or poorly-absorbed short-chain carbs
chain.lay: Your gut can't absorb the sugar, so it stays inside
chain.say: Because you've just taken in these sugars, your body now has to move them out of your **small intestine** — the long upper tube where most food is normally absorbed into your blood — and into your bloodstream. The catch is that these particular sugars are hard to absorb there. Lactose, for example, needs an **enzyme** (a tiny protein tool your body makes to break one substance into another) called **lactase** to split it before it can be taken up; many adults make very little lactase, so the lactose is never broken down. The FODMAPs have the same problem for a different reason — they're simply built in a shape your small intestine can't grab quickly. So instead of leaving through the gut wall like normal food, these sugars stay stuck inside the tube and travel onward undigested.

chain.node: Osmotic water pulled into the lumen (fast) + colonic fermentation of the carbs (slower)
chain.lay: The trapped sugar pulls in water, then bacteria ferment it and make gas
chain.say: Now that the sugar is stuck inside your gut instead of being absorbed, it causes two problems. First, sugar attracts water — this is **osmosis**, the natural pull of water toward wherever there's more dissolved stuff — so water is drawn out of your body and into the **lumen** (the hollow inside of the gut tube). This happens fast. Second, the undigested sugar keeps travelling down into your **colon** (your large intestine, the final stretch), where trillions of **bacteria** — the tiny living microbes that naturally make their home in your gut — are waiting. These bacteria treat the leftover sugar as free food and **ferment** it — break it down for energy — and fermentation releases **gas** as a by-product. This part is slower, which is why the trouble can build over several hours.

chain.node: Gas, distension, cramping, loose stools 1–8 h after the trigger food
chain.lay: Extra water and gas stretch your gut, causing bloating, cramps, and loose stools
chain.say: Because your gut is now holding both the extra water it pulled in and the gas the bacteria produced, the tube physically swells and stretches. Your gut wall is lined with stretch-sensitive nerves, so that swelling is exactly what you feel as **distension** (visible bloating), **cramping** (the squeezing ache as the gut muscles push against the pressure), and audible gurgling. The extra water also makes your stool looser and speeds it along, which is why you may get **loose stools** or a sudden urge to go. All of this typically shows up **one to eight hours** after the trigger food — the fast water pull explains the early bloating, and the slower fermentation explains why gas and discomfort can arrive later the same day.

tell.symptoms: The same foods set you off every time — dairy, wheat, onion or garlic, beans, or sweet fruit — and if you kept a simple food-and-symptom diary you would see a clear, repeatable pattern. On a plain, low-trigger day you often feel completely fine, which is the giveaway that a specific food, not your gut in general, is the problem.
tell.labMarker: Lactose or fructose hydrogen breath test positive (H2 rise ≥20 ppm over baseline); or a structured low-FODMAP elimination then reintroduction that reproduces symptoms. Before blaming wheat, exclude coeliac disease with tTG-IgA serology WHILE still eating gluten — self-treating with gluten avoidance hides it.

fixes.what: Run a dietitian-guided low-FODMAP elimination then systematic reintroduction — identify YOUR triggers, don't restrict forever (prolonged restriction itself lowers microbiome diversity)
If lactose is the culprit: switch to lactose-free dairy or take lactase with dairy
A well-tolerated soluble fibre can improve overall tolerance and stool form
Targeted probiotic strains can modestly improve carbohydrate tolerance over time

causes.confusedWith: Coeliac disease (exclude before cutting gluten) and bile-acid malabsorption (mimics FODMAP-driven IBS-D but responds to a sequestrant, not diet); SIBO and dysbiosis also ferment carbs into gas — breath testing and a clean reintroduction distinguish them
causes.keyInsight: Some everyday sugars in food never get absorbed by you — so they drag water into your gut and get eaten by your gut bacteria, and that water and gas is what bloats and cramps you a few hours after the meal.
causes.hook: Same foods bloat you every time, hours later
causes.evidence: This is a well-established mechanism (evidence tier 3): the way these particular sugars pull in water and get fermented is measurable and reproducible, and low-FODMAP eating is a mainstream, clinically studied approach. What varies is which foods bother you and how much, so your own diary matters more than any general food list.
causes.plain: Certain natural sugars in food — the milk sugar lactose, and a group of short, poorly-absorbed carbohydrates nicknamed FODMAPs found in wheat, onion, garlic, beans, and some fruit — are hard for your small intestine to absorb. Because they stay inside your gut instead of passing into your blood, they do two things: they pull water into your gut like a sponge, and they become food for the bacteria living lower down, which ferment them and release gas. The water and gas stretch your gut wall, and that stretching is what you feel as bloating, cramping, and looser stools, usually one to eight hours after eating. This isn't an allergy and it isn't dangerous — it's a plumbing-and-fermentation problem, and it eases when you eat less of the specific sugars your gut can't handle.

causes.name: SIBO / impaired motility

chain.node: Weak migrating motor complex, slow transit, or low stomach acid
chain.lay: Your gut's clean-up wave stalls
chain.say: Your **small intestine** — the long, narrow tube just after your stomach where most of your food is broken down and absorbed into your blood — is meant to stay almost free of bacteria. Keeping it that way is a housekeeping wave called the **migrating motor complex**, a slow, sweeping muscle squeeze that travels down your gut roughly every 90 minutes between meals, pushing leftover food and stray bacteria toward the exit. Two other things guard it: normal **transit** (simply the speed at which food travels through you) and **stomach acid** (the strong acid your stomach makes, which kills most bacteria as they come in). When any of these weakens — the sweeping wave turns sluggish, transit slows down, or acid runs low (often after years of acid-blocking medication) — that natural cleaning quietly stops working. Nothing hurts yet, but the door has been left open.

chain.node: Bacteria colonise the small intestine and ferment food before it's absorbed
chain.lay: Bacteria move in and eat your food first
chain.say: Because that housekeeping wave has stalled and the acid gatekeeper has gone quiet, bacteria that should have been swept onward or killed now settle in and **colonise** your small intestine — meaning they set up home and multiply in a place they don't belong. Normally your food is absorbed here before bacteria ever get a share, but now the microbes reach it first and **ferment** it — **fermentation** is simply how bacteria break down sugars and fibre without using oxygen, the same reaction that makes bread rise or beer bubble. So the **carbohydrates** from your meal — the sugars and starches that are meant to feed you — instead of quietly nourishing you become a feast for microbes sitting in the wrong location. This is precisely why the usual gut advice backfires: adding **fibre** and **probiotics** (live bacteria taken as supplements) just pours more fuel and more microbes into a stretch of gut that is already overcrowded.

chain.node: Excess hydrogen / methane / hydrogen sulfide gas and distension
chain.lay: All that feeding makes gas that inflates your gut
